## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction.md explicitly describes all the behaviors the tests verify: disabled-account detection (shadow tokens, non-interactive shells), effective group membership via primary GID, passwordless-sudo determination (NOPASSWD:ALL scope, alias/group/negation resolution, Defaults !authenticate override), service-account exemptions (roster and UID ceiling), sshd drop-in precedence rules (first-match, Match blocks, KbdInteractiveAuthentication alias), statelessness, and the audit ledger (file location, append behavior, preservation of existing records, and the directive to 'investigate the file on disk' which reveals the legacy schema). The instruction also directs the agent to Appendix G amendments, which govern the exact token sets and rules tested. All tested behaviors are derivable from the instruction plus the Standard it references.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: All behaviors described in instruction.md are tested: disabled-account detection and key revocation (test_locked_and_extended_shells_lose_keys), primary-GID group membership (test_primary_group_membership_grants_sudo), NOPASSWD:ALL scoping and negation (test_nopasswd_all_and_negation), Defaults !authenticate grant (test_sudo_defaults_authenticate_grants_passwordless), service-account exemptions including UID ceiling and roster (test_exempt_ceiling_and_roster), sshd drop-in precedence and prohibit-password (test_sshd_precedence_and_prohibit_password), KbdInteractiveAuthentication alias (test_sshd_kbdinteractive_alias_first_match), statelessness (covered by test_matches_resolved_policy_on_random_hosts which runs 60 randomized cases against a single server instance), last-match semantics (test_sudo_last_match_wins), Match all resuming global scope (test_sshd_match_all_resumes_global), includedir splicing (test_sudo_includedir_splices_in_order), ledger migration and appending (test_audit_ledger_migrated_and_records_patches), and compliant-host idempotence (test_compliant_host_needs_no_patches).
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Each test function has a descriptive docstring stating exactly what it verifies (e.g., 'Sudo granted via the primary GID (not only supplementary groups) is detected', 'First global value wins, Match blocks ignored, prohibit-password accepted'). Tests are organized as individual functions with clear fixture-based inputs and explicit expected outputs. The helper module is cleanly separated from the tests. The overall structure is readable and maintainable.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The Dockerfile does not copy tests/ or solution/ into the image—only cpp-auditor/, fixtures/, and docs/ are included. The reference implementation (expected_patches, normalize, and all helper parsing functions) lives exclusively in helpers.py which is mounted at /tests/ only at verification time, not during agent execution. The 60-case random test uses a fixed seed but against dynamically generated snapshots that the agent cannot pre-compute without implementing the policy. The ledger is reset by the test runner before tests execute, preventing pre-seeding. The agent must derive behavior from the Standard document, not from any leaked ground-truth implementation.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: The instruction explicitly documents the API schema: POST /v1/setup/audit with request body {"files": {...}} and response {"patches": [...]}. Each patch type is named: sudoers.require_password (with 'target' field), ssh.revoke_keys (with 'target' field), and systemd.set_dropin (with 'unit', 'key', 'value' fields). The ledger structure is described in the instruction and the existing on-disk file makes the schema-1 format discoverable. The schema is adequately documented for implementation.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: Python pip dependencies in requirements.txt are fully pinned with version numbers and SHA256 hashes (--require-hashes). Apt packages in the Dockerfile all have pinned version strings (e.g., g++=4:12.2.0-3, cmake=3.25.1-1). The httplib.h header-only library is pinned to v0.15.3 with a SHA256 checksum verification. Dependency pinning is thorough throughout.
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names across instruction.md, Dockerfile, test files, CMakeLists.txt, source files, fixtures, or the solution. Paths referenced in tests (e.g., /app/fixtures/, /app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json) are consistent with what the Dockerfile creates.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only COPYs cpp-auditor/, fixtures/, and docs/ into the image. Neither tests/ nor solution/ directories are copied into the runtime build. The test runner mounts /tests at verification time externally. The .dockerignore also does not inadvertently include these directories.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solution/solve.sh copies three pre-written .cpp files (parse.cpp, audit.cpp, main.cpp) into the source directory and then runs cmake to rebuild. The .cpp files contain real, non-trivial C++ implementations of all the required parsing and audit logic (sudoers parsing with alias expansion, SSHD drop-in processing with Match block handling, disabled-account detection, etc.). The solution derives the answer through genuine computation rather than echoing hardcoded output.
✅ pass - file_reference_mentioned: The task does not require the agent to produce any output files beyond modifying the C++ source code. The audit ledger path (/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json) that tests read is explicitly mentioned in instruction.md. No other output files are produced or checked by tests. The instruction states to rebuild using 'the existing build/ target' (cmake), which is consistent with what tests verify.

[Container] 2026/06/26 03:48:38.917439 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found'
Our lab access gateway runs a C++ setup auditor in `/app/cpp-auditor`. It takes a
snapshot of a host's configuration files (passwd, shadow, group, sudoers,
authorized_keys, and the sshd drop-ins) and proposes the patches needed to bring
the host into policy. It exposes `POST /v1/setup/audit` with a request body of
the form `{"files": {...}}` and replies with `{"patches": [...]}`, where each
patch is one of `sudoers.require_password` (target), `ssh.revoke_keys` (target),
or `systemd.set_dropin` (unit/key/value).

The authoritative policy is the **Lab Access Hardening Standard** in
`/app/docs/standard.md`. The auditor has fallen behind the Standard: it predates
several changes and also has a few outright parsing defects, so it now disagrees
with policy on many hosts.

The auditor's logic lives mainly in `/app/cpp-auditor/src/parse.cpp` (turns the
raw files into a normalized inventory) and `/app/cpp-auditor/src/audit.cpp`
(turns that inventory into patches). Fix the service so that, for any host
snapshot, `POST /v1/setup/audit` returns exactly the patch set the Standard
implies; a host that is already compliant must produce an empty patch set.
Rebuild with the provided CMake setup (the existing `build/` target) after
editing.

The auditor must also be **stateless**: the patch set returned for a request must
be a pure function of the snapshot in that request, unaffected by any snapshot
audited earlier in the same run. Verification audits many different hosts against
a single long-lived server, so anything that lets one host's data carry over to
another will show up as incorrect patches.

Finally, the auditor keeps a persistent **audit ledger** at
`/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json` and is supposed to append every patch it
emits. Operators have noticed the ledger is no longer being updated even though
audits keep returning results, so its record has gone stale. Restore durable
logging so that each audit's patches are appended to the ledger and earlier
records are preserved; investigate the file on disk to understand why the current
build stopped writing to it.

The behaviors the auditor must get right, all governed by the Standard, are:

- **disabled-account detection** — which `shadow` password tokens and which
  non-interactive login shells mark an account disabled;
- **effective group membership** — including members who belong via their
  *primary* group id, not just the textual member list;
- **passwordless-sudo determination** — which `sudoers` rules actually grant it
  (the scope `NOPASSWD` applies to, alias/`%group`/negation resolution, and
  per-user/group `Defaults` overrides);
- **service-account exemptions** — the exempt roster and the user-id ceiling;
- **effective sshd drop-in settings** — the fragment precedence rules and which
  keywords and values are accepted (including the keyboard-interactive keyword
  and its deprecated alias).

Read the Standard carefully. It is maintained as a body of numbered controls
followed by an **authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G)**; where the body
and an amendment conflict, the amendment governs (see section 1.4), so several
controls do not mean quite what their body text says on its own. The exact token
sets, accepted values, ceilings, and overrides are defined there as amended.

The toolchain (cmake, g++) is already installed and everything runs offline.

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite combines 60 randomized policy-compliance checks with targeted
fixture-based tests for each amended control, providing comprehensive coverage
of every core constraint with correctness assertions.

Strengths:  The randomized test generator exercises every Appendix G amendment
(last-match-wins, includedir, Match all, ChallengeResponseAuthentication alias,
Defaults !authenticate, NOPASSWD: ALL scope, extended shells/tokens, lowered UID
ceiling, extended exempt roster, without-password acceptance) across 60 varied
host configurations against a single long-lived server, which simultaneously
validates correctness AND catches statefulness bugs.

Weaknesses: None significant — the suite is well-designed for this task.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite verifies the C++ auditor against a hidden reference
implementation that faithfully encodes the Lab Access Hardening Standard as
amended by all ten auditor-relevant Appendix G clauses. Twelve targeted
fixture tests cover each individual behavior (disabled accounts, primary GID
membership, NOPASSWD: ALL scoping, negation, Defaults !authenticate,
last-match-wins, includedir splicing, sshd precedence, Match all resumption,
KbdInteractiveAuthentication aliasing, exempt ceiling/roster, and ledger
migration). The 60-case randomized property test exercises combinatorial
interactions and implicitly validates statefulness by hitting one server
instance with many distinct snapshots. The agent cannot access the reference
implementation or test fixtures from inside the container's test path, so no
shortcut is available — it must correctly implement the full amended policy
in C++ to pass.

================================================================================

                           REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires fixing a C++ security auditor that parses host configuration
snapshots (passwd, shadow, group, sudoers, sshd drop-ins) and proposes
remediation patches per a detailed hardening standard with amendments. The
agent must fix parsing defects, correct policy rules, restore statelessness,
and repair a broken audit ledger. The solution replaces three C++ source files
(parse.cpp, audit.cpp, main.cpp). The test suite validates 12 specific
scenarios plus 60 randomized host snapshots against a reference implementation
hidden from the agent.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Missing `tags` Field Length Validation
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/task.toml (line 12)
Problem: The tags array contains 5 tags which is within the valid range (3-6),
         but the tag "long-context" partially duplicates the subcategory
         "long_context" already declared on line 8. This is minor redundancy.

Current approach: Tags include both "long-context" and the subcategory
"long_context" is also declared.

Suggested fix: Consider replacing the "long-context" tag with a more
descriptive tag like "cpp" or "parsing" to add information value:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  tags = ["security", "sudoers", "sshd", "cpp", "debugging"]                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Tags should provide additional discovery dimensions beyond what
subcategories already convey. Duplicating the subcategory as a tag wastes a
slot that could improve discoverability.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Dockerfile Fetches External File at Build Time Without Fallback
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (lines 34-38)
Problem: The Dockerfile downloads cpp-httplib from GitHub at build time. While
         build-time network is allowed, this introduces a dependency on an
         external URL that could become unavailable, breaking future builds.

Current approach: Downloads httplib.h from a GitHub raw URL with SHA256
verification at build time.

Suggested fix: Consider vendoring httplib.h into the environment/ directory
to eliminate the external dependency entirely:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  COPY cpp-auditor/include/httplib.h /app/cpp-auditor/include/httplib.h      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Although build-time downloads are permitted, vendoring the file
ensures the task remains buildable even if the GitHub URL goes offline. The
file is already checksum-pinned so integrity is maintained either way.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Consider Adding `set -e` to test.sh
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test.sh (line 2)

Current approach: Uses `set -uo pipefail` but omits `-e`. The script handles
exit codes manually, which is intentional for the cmake build check, but
could mask unexpected failures in the setup portions.

Suggested improvement: The current approach is defensible since the script
needs fine-grained exit code control. No change strictly required — this is
just a note that the omission appears intentional and well-handled.

Rationale: The script correctly captures the cmake build exit code and writes
reward 0 on failure, so the manual handling is appropriate here.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is a well-crafted, challenging security-engineering task that tests deep
C++ debugging, policy interpretation from a dense specification with amendments,
and systems-level reasoning about statefulness and file format migration.
The task is realistic and well-structured with no critical issues.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Excellent test coverage: 12 deterministic fixture tests plus 60 randomized
    snapshots ensure comprehensive behavioral validation
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating design: reference implementation hidden in test
    helpers, policy derived from a complex standard with amendments
  ✓ Multi-layered challenge: parsing bugs, rule defects, statefulness issue,
    and ledger migration all require distinct debugging skills

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ External build-time dependency on GitHub for httplib.h (minor fragility)
  ✗ Tag/subcategory redundancy (cosmetic)

Evaluates: C++ debugging, security policy interpretation, stateful service
           analysis, long-context document comprehension

## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction.md explicitly describes all the behaviors the tests verify: disabled-account detection (shadow tokens, non-interactive shells), effective group membership via primary GID, passwordless-sudo determination (NOPASSWD:ALL scope, alias/group/negation resolution, Defaults !authenticate override), service-account exemptions (roster and UID ceiling), sshd drop-in precedence rules (first-match, Match blocks, KbdInteractiveAuthentication alias), statelessness, and the audit ledger (file location, append behavior, preservation of existing records, and the directive to 'investigate the file on disk' which reveals the legacy schema). The instruction also directs the agent to Appendix G amendments, which govern the exact token sets and rules tested. All tested behaviors are derivable from the instruction plus the Standard it references.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: All behaviors described in instruction.md are tested: disabled-account detection and key revocation (test_locked_and_extended_shells_lose_keys), primary-GID group membership (test_primary_group_membership_grants_sudo), NOPASSWD:ALL scoping and negation (test_nopasswd_all_and_negation), Defaults !authenticate grant (test_sudo_defaults_authenticate_grants_passwordless), service-account exemptions including UID ceiling and roster (test_exempt_ceiling_and_roster), sshd drop-in precedence and prohibit-password (test_sshd_precedence_and_prohibit_password), KbdInteractiveAuthentication alias (test_sshd_kbdinteractive_alias_first_match), statelessness (covered by test_matches_resolved_policy_on_random_hosts which runs 60 randomized cases against a single server instance), last-match semantics (test_sudo_last_match_wins), Match all resuming global scope (test_sshd_match_all_resumes_global), includedir splicing (test_sudo_includedir_splices_in_order), ledger migration and appending (test_audit_ledger_migrated_and_records_patches), and compliant-host idempotence (test_compliant_host_needs_no_patches).
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Each test function has a descriptive docstring stating exactly what it verifies (e.g., 'Sudo granted via the primary GID (not only supplementary groups) is detected', 'First global value wins, Match blocks ignored, prohibit-password accepted'). Tests are organized as individual functions with clear fixture-based inputs and explicit expected outputs. The helper module is cleanly separated from the tests. The overall structure is readable and maintainable.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The Dockerfile does not copy tests/ or solution/ into the image—only cpp-auditor/, fixtures/, and docs/ are included. The reference implementation (expected_patches, normalize, and all helper parsing functions) lives exclusively in helpers.py which is mounted at /tests/ only at verification time, not during agent execution. The 60-case random test uses a fixed seed but against dynamically generated snapshots that the agent cannot pre-compute without implementing the policy. The ledger is reset by the test runner before tests execute, preventing pre-seeding. The agent must derive behavior from the Standard document, not from any leaked ground-truth implementation.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: The instruction explicitly documents the API schema: POST /v1/setup/audit with request body {"files": {...}} and response {"patches": [...]}. Each patch type is named: sudoers.require_password (with 'target' field), ssh.revoke_keys (with 'target' field), and systemd.set_dropin (with 'unit', 'key', 'value' fields). The ledger structure is described in the instruction and the existing on-disk file makes the schema-1 format discoverable. The schema is adequately documented for implementation.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: Python pip dependencies in requirements.txt are fully pinned with version numbers and SHA256 hashes (--require-hashes). Apt packages in the Dockerfile all have pinned version strings (e.g., g++=4:12.2.0-3, cmake=3.25.1-1). The httplib.h header-only library is pinned to v0.15.3 with a SHA256 checksum verification. Dependency pinning is thorough throughout.
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names across instruction.md, Dockerfile, test files, CMakeLists.txt, source files, fixtures, or the solution. Paths referenced in tests (e.g., /app/fixtures/, /app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json) are consistent with what the Dockerfile creates.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only COPYs cpp-auditor/, fixtures/, and docs/ into the image. Neither tests/ nor solution/ directories are copied into the runtime build. The test runner mounts /tests at verification time externally. The .dockerignore also does not inadvertently include these directories.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solution/solve.sh copies three pre-written .cpp files (parse.cpp, audit.cpp, main.cpp) into the source directory and then runs cmake to rebuild. The .cpp files contain real, non-trivial C++ implementations of all the required parsing and audit logic (sudoers parsing with alias expansion, SSHD drop-in processing with Match block handling, disabled-account detection, etc.). The solution derives the answer through genuine computation rather than echoing hardcoded output.
✅ pass - file_reference_mentioned: The task does not require the agent to produce any output files beyond modifying the C++ source code. The audit ledger path (/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json) that tests read is explicitly mentioned in instruction.md. No other output files are produced or checked by tests. [Container] 2026/06/26 03:48:38.917439 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found'
Our lab access gateway runs a C++ setup auditor in `/app/cpp-auditor`. It takes a
snapshot of a host's configuration files (passwd, shadow, group, sudoers,
authorized_keys, and the sshd drop-ins) and proposes the patches needed to bring
the host into policy. It exposes `POST /v1/setup/audit` with a request body of
the form `{"files": {...}}` and replies with `{"patches": [...]}`, where each
patch is one of `sudoers.require_password` (target), `ssh.revoke_keys` (target),
or `systemd.set_dropin` (unit/key/value).

The authoritative policy is the **Lab Access Hardening Standard** in
`/app/docs/standard.md`. The auditor has fallen behind the Standard: it predates
several changes and also has a few outright parsing defects, so it now disagrees
with policy on many hosts.

The auditor's logic lives mainly in `/app/cpp-auditor/src/parse.cpp` (turns the
raw files into a normalized inventory) and `/app/cpp-auditor/src/audit.cpp`
(turns that inventory into patches). Fix the service so that, for any host
snapshot, `POST /v1/setup/audit` returns exactly the patch set the Standard
implies; a host that is already compliant must produce an empty patch set.
Rebuild with the provided CMake setup (the existing `build/` target) after
editing.

The auditor must also be **stateless**: the patch set returned for a request must
be a pure function of the snapshot in that request, unaffected by any snapshot
audited earlier in the same run. Verification audits many different hosts against
a single long-lived server, so anything that lets one host's data carry over to
another will show up as incorrect patches.

Finally, the auditor keeps a persistent **audit ledger** at
`/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json` and is supposed to append every patch it
emits. Operators have noticed the ledger is no longer being updated even though
audits keep returning results, so its record has gone stale. Restore durable
logging so that each audit's patches are appended to the ledger and earlier
records are preserved; investigate the file on disk to understand why the current
build stopped writing to it.

The behaviors the auditor must get right, all governed by the Standard, are:

- **disabled-account detection** — which `shadow` password tokens and which
  non-interactive login shells mark an account disabled;
- **effective group membership** — including members who belong via their
  *primary* group id, not just the textual member list;
- **passwordless-sudo determination** — which `sudoers` rules actually grant it
  (the scope `NOPASSWD` applies to, alias/`%group`/negation resolution, and
  per-user/group `Defaults` overrides);
- **service-account exemptions** — the exempt roster and the user-id ceiling;
- **effective sshd drop-in settings** — the fragment precedence rules and which
  keywords and values are accepted (including the keyboard-interactive keyword
  and its deprecated alias).

Read the Standard carefully. It is maintained as a body of numbered controls
followed by an **authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G)**; where the body
and an amendment conflict, the amendment governs (see section 1.4), so several
controls do not mean quite what their body text says on its own. The exact token
sets, accepted values, ceilings, and overrides are defined there as amended.

The toolchain (cmake, g++) is already installed and everything runs offline.

2026-06-26 03:49:52,017 INFO scripts.test_quality_judge.reviewer: [tbench-task] agent finished (15 turns)
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite combines 60 randomized policy-compliance checks with targeted
fixture-based tests for each amended control, providing comprehensive coverage
of every core constraint with correctness assertions.

Strengths:  The randomized test generator exercises every Appendix G amendment
(last-match-wins, includedir, Match all, ChallengeResponseAuthentication alias,
Defaults !authenticate, NOPASSWD: ALL scope, extended shells/tokens, lowered UID
ceiling, extended exempt roster, without-password acceptance) across 60 varied
host configurations against a single long-lived server, which simultaneously
validates correctness AND catches statefulness bugs.

Weaknesses: None significant — the suite is well-designed for this task.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite verifies the C++ auditor against a hidden reference
implementation that faithfully encodes the Lab Access Hardening Standard as
amended by all ten auditor-relevant Appendix G clauses. Twelve targeted
fixture tests cover each individual behavior (disabled accounts, primary GID
membership, NOPASSWD: ALL scoping, negation, Defaults !authenticate,
last-match-wins, includedir splicing, sshd precedence, Match all resumption,
KbdInteractiveAuthentication aliasing, exempt ceiling/roster, and ledger
migration). The 60-case randomized property test exercises combinatorial
interactions and implicitly validates statefulness by hitting one server
instance with many distinct snapshots. The agent cannot access the reference
implementation or test fixtures from inside the container's test path, so no
shortcut is available — it must correctly implement the full amended policy
in C++ to pass.

================================================================================

<task-notification>Task wb71zoaw9 completed</task-notification>
## Benchmark Job Summary

**Task:** C++ auditor fixing (Lab Access Hardening Standard compliance + ledger management)
**Trials:** 9 | **Date:** 2026-06-26

---

## 1. Overall Results

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Total trials | 9 |
| Passed (reward=1) | **0** |
| Failed (reward=0) | **9** |
| Avg test-suite completion | ~78% |
| Trials at 13/14 tests | 5 |
| Infrastructure failures | 1 (uninformative) |

**Scoring note:** Binary reward masks near-complete solutions. Five trials scored 13/14 and failed a single undocumented requirement; they are functionally different from the 1/14 infrastructure failure, but both count as reward=0 in aggregate.

---

## 2. Common Failure Patterns

| Pattern | Frequency | Affected Trials |
|---|---|---|
| Ledger schema migration not implemented | **7/9** | Gvp5upY, fDSG2pJ, aX2HM3K, jWM7DgP, mTFW4xb, rWPAPZn, zYWqMFH |
| Randomized snapshot test failures | 3/9 | Gvp5upY (~20/60), 6nxskru (12/60), zYWqMFH (7/60) |
| Infrastructure failure / server restart | 1/9 | u3zpses |
| Missing G-2026-10 amendment (PermitRootLogin) | 1/9 | 6nxskru |
| Disabled-account detection incomplete | 1/9 | Gvp5upY |
| `#includedir` directive test failure | 1/9 | zYWqMFH |

**Ledger migration (dominant failure):** The test `test_audit_ledger_migrated_and_records_patches` requires migrating persistent storage from schema 1 (`audits` key) → schema 2 (`entries` key, `schema=2`). This requirement was entirely absent from the task spec. Every affected agent diagnosed the underlying bug (`.at("entries")` throwing on a schema-1 file) but "fixed" it by reverting to schema 1 rather than completing the intended migration. Note: jWM7DgP also manually reset the ledger at run-end, compounding the failure.

**Snapshot failures:** Partial, not total — indicating conditionally correct policy logic rather than a single missing feature. Gvp5upY is the most severe case.

**Infrastructure failure (u3zpses):** Timed out at 1200s because the live server was PID-1 and couldn't be restarted. Only 1/14 tests passed. **Exclude from capability analysis** — this is an environment-level failure.

---

## 3. Hack Check

✅ **All 9 trials passed.** No trial attempted reward hacking — no test-harness stubbing, test-file modification, hardcoded outputs, or evaluation-pipeline manipulation detected. The honest-failure rate confirms the benchmark is measuring genuine capability, not gaming behavior.

---

## 4. Instruction / Specification Issues

| Issue | Severity | Affected Trials | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ledger migration requirement undocumented | **Critical** | 7/9 | Add explicit spec section: agent must migrate from schema 1 (`audits`) to schema 2 (`entries`, `schema=2`). This single omission caused 7/9 failures. |
| G-2026-10 amendment (PermitRootLogin `without-password`) may be overlooked | Moderate | 6nxskru | Verify prominence in Appendix G; add a required-amendments checklist to the task preamble. |
| Binary-only scoring hides partial progress | Minor | fDSG2pJ, aX2HM3K, jWM7DgP, mTFW4xb, rWPAPZn | Record a continuous secondary score (tests_passed/total) alongside reward — a 13/14 trial is not equivalent to a 1/14 trial. |

---

## 5. Progress Assessment

**Average completion: ~78% of tests passed.** The dominant story is near-success blocked by a spec gap.

- **Best performers** — 13/14 tests passed, failed only on undocumented ledger migration: fDSG2pJ, aX2HM3K, jWM7DgP, mTFW4xb, rWPAPZn
- **Mid-tier** — 6nxskru: 12/14, missed G-2026-10 + partial snapshot failures
- **Worst performers:**
  - Gvp5upY: broadest failure set — ledger, ~20/60 snapshot failures, missing `dan` in disabled-account detection
  - u3zpses: 1/14, environment failure, no capability signal

**Projection:** Documenting the ledger migration requirement and fixing the PID-1 restart issue would likely move **5–6 of 9 trials to reward=1** on the next run with no model changes.

---

## 6. Model & Agent Differences

| Trial | Model | Score |
|---|---|---|
| mTFW4xb | Claude Opus 4.8 (confirmed) | 13/14 |
| fDSG2pJ, aX2HM3K, jWM7DgP, rWPAPZn | Unknown | 13/14 |
| 6nxskru | Unknown | 12/14 |
| zYWqMFH | Unknown | ~11/14 |
| Gvp5upY | Unknown | ~8/14 |
| u3zpses | Unknown | 1/14 (infra failure) |

Only one trial (mTFW4xb) has confirmed model identity. Claude Opus 4.8 performed at 13/14, indistinguishable from the four other 13/14 trials of unknown provenance — no meaningful model comparison is possible. **Action required: instrument trial logging to record model ID** before any model-level analysis can be conducted.

[Container] 2026/06/26 03:48:32.805367 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found; task may use steps/*/instruction.md'
Our lab access gateway runs a C++ setup auditor in `/app/cpp-auditor`. It takes a
snapshot of a host's configuration files (passwd, shadow, group, sudoers,
authorized_keys, and the sshd drop-ins) and proposes the patches needed to bring
the host into policy. It exposes `POST /v1/setup/audit` with a request body of
the form `{"files": {...}}` and replies with `{"patches": [...]}`, where each
patch is one of `sudoers.require_password` (target), `ssh.revoke_keys` (target),
or `systemd.set_dropin` (unit/key/value).

The authoritative policy is the **Lab Access Hardening Standard** in
`/app/docs/standard.md`. The auditor has fallen behind the Standard: it predates
several changes and also has a few outright parsing defects, so it now disagrees
with policy on many hosts.

The auditor's logic lives mainly in `/app/cpp-auditor/src/parse.cpp` (turns the
raw files into a normalized inventory) and `/app/cpp-auditor/src/audit.cpp`
(turns that inventory into patches). Fix the service so that, for any host
snapshot, `POST /v1/setup/audit` returns exactly the patch set the Standard
implies; a host that is already compliant must produce an empty patch set.
Rebuild with the provided CMake setup (the existing `build/` target) after
editing.

The auditor must also be **stateless**: the patch set returned for a request must
be a pure function of the snapshot in that request, unaffected by any snapshot
audited earlier in the same run. Verification audits many different hosts against
a single long-lived server, so anything that lets one host's data carry over to
another will show up as incorrect patches.

Finally, the auditor keeps a persistent **audit ledger** at
`/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json` and is supposed to append every patch it
emits. Operators have noticed the ledger is no longer being updated even though
audits keep returning results, so its record has gone stale. Restore durable
logging so that each audit's patches are appended to the ledger and earlier
records are preserved; investigate the file on disk to understand why the current
build stopped writing to it.

The behaviors the auditor must get right, all governed by the Standard, are:

- **disabled-account detection** — which `shadow` password tokens and which
  non-interactive login shells mark an account disabled;
- **effective group membership** — including members who belong via their
  *primary* group id, not just the textual member list;
- **passwordless-sudo determination** — which `sudoers` rules actually grant it
  (the scope `NOPASSWD` applies to, alias/`%group`/negation resolution, and
  per-user/group `Defaults` overrides);
- **service-account exemptions** — the exempt roster and the user-id ceiling;
- **effective sshd drop-in settings** — the fragment precedence rules and which
  keywords and values are accepted (including the keyboard-interactive keyword
  and its deprecated alias).

Read the Standard carefully. It is maintained as a body of numbered controls
followed by an **authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G)**; where the body
and an amendment conflict, the amendment governs (see section 1.4), so several
controls do not mean quite what their body text says on its own. The exact token
sets, accepted values, ceilings, and overrides are defined there as amended.

The toolchain (cmake, g++) is already installed and everything runs offline.

                           REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires fixing a C++ security auditor that parses host configuration
snapshots (passwd, shadow, group, sudoers, sshd drop-ins) and proposes
remediation patches per a detailed hardening standard with amendments. The
agent must fix parsing defects, correct policy rules, restore statelessness,
and repair a broken audit ledger. The solution replaces three C++ source files
(parse.cpp, audit.cpp, main.cpp). The test suite validates 12 specific
scenarios plus 60 randomized host snapshots against a reference implementation
hidden from the agent.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Missing `tags` Field Length Validation
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/task.toml (line 12)
Problem: The tags array contains 5 tags which is within the valid range (3-6),
         but the tag "long-context" partially duplicates the subcategory
         "long_context" already declared on line 8. This is minor redundancy.

Current approach: Tags include both "long-context" and the subcategory
"long_context" is also declared.

Suggested fix: Consider replacing the "long-context" tag with a more
descriptive tag like "cpp" or "parsing" to add information value:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  tags = ["security", "sudoers", "sshd", "cpp", "debugging"]                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Tags should provide additional discovery dimensions beyond what
subcategories already convey. Duplicating the subcategory as a tag wastes a
slot that could improve discoverability.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Dockerfile Fetches External File at Build Time Without Fallback
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (lines 34-38)
Problem: The Dockerfile downloads cpp-httplib from GitHub at build time. While
         build-time network is allowed, this introduces a dependency on an
         external URL that could become unavailable, breaking future builds.

Current approach: Downloads httplib.h from a GitHub raw URL with SHA256
verification at build time.

Suggested fix: Consider vendoring httplib.h into the environment/ directory
to eliminate the external dependency entirely:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  COPY cpp-auditor/include/httplib.h /app/cpp-auditor/include/httplib.h      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Although build-time downloads are permitted, vendoring the file
ensures the task remains buildable even if the GitHub URL goes offline. The
file is already checksum-pinned so integrity is maintained either way.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Consider Adding `set -e` to test.sh
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test.sh (line 2)

Current approach: Uses `set -uo pipefail` but omits `-e`. The script handles
exit codes manually, which is intentional for the cmake build check, but
could mask unexpected failures in the setup portions.

Suggested improvement: The current approach is defensible since the script
needs fine-grained exit code control. No change strictly required — this is
just a note that the omission appears intentional and well-handled.

Rationale: The script correctly captures the cmake build exit code and writes
reward 0 on failure, so the manual handling is appropriate here.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is a well-crafted, challenging security-engineering task that tests deep
C++ debugging, policy interpretation from a dense specification with amendments,
and systems-level reasoning about statefulness and file format migration.
The task is realistic and well-structured with no critical issues.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Excellent test coverage: 12 deterministic fixture tests plus 60 randomized
    snapshots ensure comprehensive behavioral validation
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating design: reference implementation hidden in test
    helpers, policy derived from a complex standard with amendments
  ✓ Multi-layered challenge: parsing bugs, rule defects, statefulness issue,
    and ledger migration all require distinct debugging skills

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ External build-time dependency on GitHub for httplib.h (minor fragility)
  ✗ Tag/subcategory redundancy (cosmetic)

Evaluates: C++ debugging, security policy interpretation, stateful service
           analysis, long-context document comprehension

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ✅ READY TO USE

  The task is well-designed with proper structure, comprehensive tests, strong
  anti-cheating measures, and appropriate difficulty. The warnings are minor
  and do not affect task correctness or agent evaluation.

================================================================================
                           REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires fixing a C++ security auditor that parses host configuration
snapshots (passwd, shadow, group, sudoers, sshd drop-ins) and proposes
remediation patches per a detailed hardening standard with amendments. The
agent must fix parsing defects, correct policy rules, restore statelessness,
and repair a broken audit ledger. The solution replaces three C++ source files
(parse.cpp, audit.cpp, main.cpp). The test suite validates 12 specific
scenarios plus 60 randomized host snapshots against a reference implementation
hidden from the agent.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Missing `tags` Field Length Validation
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/task.toml (line 12)
Problem: The tags array contains 5 tags which is within the valid range (3-6),
         but the tag "long-context" partially duplicates the subcategory
         "long_context" already declared on line 8. This is minor redundancy.

Current approach: Tags include both "long-context" and the subcategory
"long_context" is also declared.

Suggested fix: Consider replacing the "long-context" tag with a more
descriptive tag like "cpp" or "parsing" to add information value:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  tags = ["security", "sudoers", "sshd", "cpp", "debugging"]                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Tags should provide additional discovery dimensions beyond what
subcategories already convey. Duplicating the subcategory as a tag wastes a
slot that could improve discoverability.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Dockerfile Fetches External File at Build Time Without Fallback
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (lines 34-38)
Problem: The Dockerfile downloads cpp-httplib from GitHub at build time. While
         build-time network is allowed, this introduces a dependency on an
         external URL that could become unavailable, breaking future builds.

Current approach: Downloads httplib.h from a GitHub raw URL with SHA256
verification at build time.

Suggested fix: Consider vendoring httplib.h into the environment/ directory
to eliminate the external dependency entirely:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  COPY cpp-auditor/include/httplib.h /app/cpp-auditor/include/httplib.h      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Although build-time downloads are permitted, vendoring the file
ensures the task remains buildable even if the GitHub URL goes offline. The
file is already checksum-pinned so integrity is maintained either way.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Consider Adding `set -e` to test.sh
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test.sh (line 2)

Current approach: Uses `set -uo pipefail` but omits `-e`. The script handles
exit codes manually, which is intentional for the cmake build check, but
could mask unexpected failures in the setup portions.

Suggested improvement: The current approach is defensible since the script
needs fine-grained exit code control. No change strictly required — this is
just a note that the omission appears intentional and well-handled.

Rationale: The script correctly captures the cmake build exit code and writes
reward 0 on failure, so the manual handling is appropriate here.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is a well-crafted, challenging security-engineering task that tests deep
C++ debugging, policy interpretation from a dense specification with amendments,
and systems-level reasoning about statefulness and file format migration.
The task is realistic and well-structured with no critical issues.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Excellent test coverage: 12 deterministic fixture tests plus 60 randomized
    snapshots ensure comprehensive behavioral validation
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating design: reference implementation hidden in test
    helpers, policy derived from a complex standard with amendments
  ✓ Multi-layered challenge: parsing bugs, rule defects, statefulness issue,
    and ledger migration all require distinct debugging skills

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ External build-time dependency on GitHub for httplib.h (minor fragility)
  ✗ Tag/subcategory redundancy (cosmetic)

Evaluates: C++ debugging, security policy interpretation, stateful service
           analysis, long-context document comprehension

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ✅ READY TO USE

  The task is well-designed with proper structure, comprehensive tests, strong
  anti-cheating measures, and appropriate difficulty. The warnings are minor
  and do not affect task correctness or agent evaluation.
================================================================================

[Container] 2026/06/26 03:48:32.805367 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found; task may use steps/*/instruction.md'
Our lab access gateway runs a C++ setup auditor in `/app/cpp-auditor`. It takes a
snapshot of a host's configuration files (passwd, shadow, group, sudoers,
authorized_keys, and the sshd drop-ins) and proposes the patches needed to bring
the host into policy. It exposes `POST /v1/setup/audit` with a request body of
the form `{"files": {...}}` and replies with `{"patches": [...]}`, where each
patch is one of `sudoers.require_password` (target), `ssh.revoke_keys` (target),
or `systemd.set_dropin` (unit/key/value).

The authoritative policy is the **Lab Access Hardening Standard** in
`/app/docs/standard.md`. The auditor has fallen behind the Standard: it predates
several changes and also has a few outright parsing defects, so it now disagrees
with policy on many hosts.

The auditor's logic lives mainly in `/app/cpp-auditor/src/parse.cpp` (turns the
raw files into a normalized inventory) and `/app/cpp-auditor/src/audit.cpp`
(turns that inventory into patches). Fix the service so that, for any host
snapshot, `POST /v1/setup/audit` returns exactly the patch set the Standard
implies; a host that is already compliant must produce an empty patch set.
Rebuild with the provided CMake setup (the existing `build/` target) after
editing.

The auditor must also be **stateless**: the patch set returned for a request must
be a pure function of the snapshot in that request, unaffected by any snapshot
audited earlier in the same run. Verification audits many different hosts against
a single long-lived server, so anything that lets one host's data carry over to
another will show up as incorrect patches.

Finally, the auditor keeps a persistent **audit ledger** at
`/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json` and is supposed to append every patch it
emits. Operators have noticed the ledger is no longer being updated even though
audits keep returning results, so its record has gone stale. Restore durable
logging so that each audit's patches are appended to the ledger and earlier
records are preserved; investigate the file on disk to understand why the current
build stopped writing to it.

The behaviors the auditor must get right, all governed by the Standard, are:

- **disabled-account detection** — which `shadow` password tokens and which
  non-interactive login shells mark an account disabled;
- **effective group membership** — including members who belong via their
  *primary* group id, not just the textual member list;
- **passwordless-sudo determination** — which `sudoers` rules actually grant it
  (the scope `NOPASSWD` applies to, alias/`%group`/negation resolution, and
  per-user/group `Defaults` overrides);
- **service-account exemptions** — the exempt roster and the user-id ceiling;
- **effective sshd drop-in settings** — the fragment precedence rules and which
  keywords and values are accepted (including the keyboard-interactive keyword
  and its deprecated alias).

Read the Standard carefully. It is maintained as a body of numbered controls
followed by an **authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G)**; where the body
and an amendment conflict, the amendment governs (see section 1.4), so several
controls do not mean quite what their body text says on its own. The exact token
sets, accepted values, ceilings, and overrides are defined there as amended.

The toolchain (cmake, g++) is already installed and everything runs offline.

# Rubric 1
Agent reads the Lab Access Hardening Standard (standard.md) including Appendix G amendments before making code changes, +3
Agent identifies and fixes the non-interactive shell set to include /usr/bin/git-shell and /bin/sync (G-2026-01 amendment), +2
Agent adds *LK* to the locked-password token set in is_disabled (G-2026-01 amendment), +1
Agent implements primary-GID group membership in effective_group_members (AC-GROUP-EFFECTIVE), +3
Agent fixes NOPASSWD detection to require NOPASSWD applied to ALL (NOPASSWD: ALL) rather than any occurrence of NOPASSWD (G-2026-02), +3
Agent implements last-match-wins semantics for sudoers passwordless determination (G-2026-07), +3
Agent implements Defaults:binder !authenticate as granting NOPASSWD: ALL to the bound principal (G-2026-05), +2
Agent implements #includedir/@includedir resolution splicing sudoers.d files inline in sorted order (G-2026-09), +2
Agent implements sudoers negation handling where negated principals are excluded from the resolved set, +2
Agent fixes sshd drop-in parsing to use first-occurrence-wins for global keywords instead of last-occurrence-wins, +3
Agent implements Match block handling: lines after a non-all Match are excluded, Match all resumes global scope (G-2026-08), +2
Agent folds ChallengeResponseAuthentication as a deprecated alias of KbdInteractiveAuthentication (G-2026-06), +2
Agent adds KbdInteractiveAuthentication to the set of evaluated sshd keywords in audit.cpp, +2
Agent accepts prohibit-password and without-password as valid values for PermitRootLogin only (G-2026-04 and G-2026-10), +2
Agent lowers the service-account UID ceiling from 999 to 499 and adds svc_backup to the exempt roster (G-2026-03), +2
Agent removes or disables the process-wide g_registry in main.cpp to make audits stateless across requests, +5
Agent fixes the audit ledger by handling the legacy schema-1 format (data under audits key) and migrating to schema-2 while preserving existing records, +3
Agent successfully rebuilds the project with cmake after making changes, +2
Agent tests the service by sending sample POST requests to verify correct behavior after changes, +1

                           REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires fixing a C++ security auditor that parses host configuration
snapshots (passwd, shadow, group, sudoers, sshd drop-ins) and proposes
remediation patches per a detailed hardening standard with amendments. The
agent must fix parsing defects, correct policy rules, restore statelessness,
and repair a broken audit ledger. The solution replaces three C++ source files
(parse.cpp, audit.cpp, main.cpp). The test suite validates 12 specific
scenarios plus 60 randomized host snapshots against a reference implementation
hidden from the agent.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Missing `tags` Field Length Validation
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/task.toml (line 12)
Problem: The tags array contains 5 tags which is within the valid range (3-6),
         but the tag "long-context" partially duplicates the subcategory
         "long_context" already declared on line 8. This is minor redundancy.

Current approach: Tags include both "long-context" and the subcategory
"long_context" is also declared.

Suggested fix: Consider replacing the "long-context" tag with a more
descriptive tag like "cpp" or "parsing" to add information value:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  tags = ["security", "sudoers", "sshd", "cpp", "debugging"]                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Tags should provide additional discovery dimensions beyond what
subcategories already convey. Duplicating the subcategory as a tag wastes a
slot that could improve discoverability.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Dockerfile Fetches External File at Build Time Without Fallback
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (lines 34-38)
Problem: The Dockerfile downloads cpp-httplib from GitHub at build time. While
         build-time network is allowed, this introduces a dependency on an
         external URL that could become unavailable, breaking future builds.

Current approach: Downloads httplib.h from a GitHub raw URL with SHA256
verification at build time.

Suggested fix: Consider vendoring httplib.h into the environment/ directory
to eliminate the external dependency entirely:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  COPY cpp-auditor/include/httplib.h /app/cpp-auditor/include/httplib.h      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Although build-time downloads are permitted, vendoring the file
ensures the task remains buildable even if the GitHub URL goes offline. The
file is already checksum-pinned so integrity is maintained either way.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Consider Adding `set -e` to test.sh
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test.sh (line 2)

Current approach: Uses `set -uo pipefail` but omits `-e`. The script handles
exit codes manually, which is intentional for the cmake build check, but
could mask unexpected failures in the setup portions.

Suggested improvement: The current approach is defensible since the script
needs fine-grained exit code control. No change strictly required — this is
just a note that the omission appears intentional and well-handled.

Rationale: The script correctly captures the cmake build exit code and writes
reward 0 on failure, so the manual handling is appropriate here.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is a well-crafted, challenging security-engineering task that tests deep
C++ debugging, policy interpretation from a dense specification with amendments,
and systems-level reasoning about statefulness and file format migration.
The task is realistic and well-structured with no critical issues.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Excellent test coverage: 12 deterministic fixture tests plus 60 randomized
    snapshots ensure comprehensive behavioral validation
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating design: reference implementation hidden in test
    helpers, policy derived from a complex standard with amendments
  ✓ Multi-layered challenge: parsing bugs, rule defects, statefulness issue,
    and ledger migration all require distinct debugging skills

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ External build-time dependency on GitHub for httplib.h (minor fragility)
  ✗ Tag/subcategory redundancy (cosmetic)

Evaluates: C++ debugging, security policy interpretation, stateful service
           analysis, long-context document comprehension

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ✅ READY TO USE

  The task is well-designed with proper structure, comprehensive tests, strong
  anti-cheating measures, and appropriate difficulty. The warnings are minor
  and do not affect task correctness or agent evaluation.

[Container] 2026/06/26 05:14:41.682041 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found'
Our lab access gateway runs a C++ setup auditor in `/app/cpp-auditor`. It takes a
snapshot of a host's configuration files (passwd, shadow, group, sudoers,
authorized_keys, and the sshd drop-ins) and proposes the patches needed to bring
the host into policy. It exposes `POST /v1/setup/audit` with a request body of
the form `{"files": {...}}` and replies with `{"patches": [...]}`, where each
patch is one of `sudoers.require_password` (target), `ssh.revoke_keys` (target),
or `systemd.set_dropin` (unit/key/value).

The authoritative policy is the **Lab Access Hardening Standard** in
`/app/docs/standard.md`. The auditor has fallen behind the Standard: it predates
several changes and also has a few outright parsing defects, so it now disagrees
with policy on many hosts.

The auditor's logic lives mainly in `/app/cpp-auditor/src/parse.cpp` (turns the
raw files into a normalized inventory) and `/app/cpp-auditor/src/audit.cpp`
(turns that inventory into patches). Fix the service so that, for any host
snapshot, `POST /v1/setup/audit` returns exactly the patch set the Standard
implies; a host that is already compliant must produce an empty patch set.
Rebuild with the provided CMake setup (the existing `build/` target) after
editing.

The auditor must also be **stateless**: the patch set returned for a request must
be a pure function of the snapshot in that request, unaffected by any snapshot
audited earlier in the same run. Verification audits many different hosts against
a single long-lived server, so anything that lets one host's data carry over to
another will show up as incorrect patches.

Finally, the auditor keeps a persistent **audit ledger** at
`/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json` and is supposed to append every patch it
emits. Operators have noticed the ledger is no longer being updated even though
audits keep returning results, so its record has gone stale. Restore durable
logging so that each audit's patches are appended to the ledger and earlier
records are preserved; investigate the file on disk to understand why the current
build stopped writing to it.

The behaviors the auditor must get right, all governed by the Standard, are:

- **disabled-account detection** — which `shadow` password tokens and which
  non-interactive login shells mark an account disabled;
- **effective group membership** — including members who belong via their
  *primary* group id, not just the textual member list;
- **passwordless-sudo determination** — which `sudoers` rules actually grant it
  (the scope `NOPASSWD` applies to, alias/`%group`/negation resolution, and
  per-user/group `Defaults` overrides);
- **service-account exemptions** — the exempt roster and the user-id ceiling;
- **effective sshd drop-in settings** — the fragment precedence rules and which
  keywords and values are accepted (including the keyboard-interactive keyword
  and its deprecated alias).

Read the Standard carefully. It is maintained as a body of numbered controls
followed by an **authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G)**; where the body
and an amendment conflict, the amendment governs (see section 1.4), so several
controls do not mean quite what their body text says on its own. The exact token
sets, accepted values, ceilings, and overrides are defined there as amended.

The toolchain (cmake, g++) is already installed and everything runs offline.

2026-06-26 05:16:22,204 INFO scripts.test_quality_judge.reviewer: [tbench-task] agent finished (20 turns)
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite provides comprehensive coverage of all core constraints through
targeted fixture tests, a 60-scenario randomized property check against a
faithful reference implementation, and implicit verification of statefulness
requirements.

Strengths:  The combination of deterministic fixture tests for each amended
control and a randomized property-based test against a hidden reference
implementation makes shortcutting effectively impossible. The reference
implementation in helpers.py faithfully encodes every clause of the standard
as amended by Appendix G.

Weaknesses: The statefulness requirement is tested only implicitly through
the randomized test running 60 requests against one server instance; a
dedicated test that explicitly verifies cross-request isolation would make
failures more diagnosable.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite verifies 14 distinct scenarios across all instruction
constraints: disabled-account detection (locked tokens, extended shells),
primary-group membership, NOPASSWD:ALL narrowing, Defaults:!authenticate
grants, last-match-wins semantics, includedir splicing, sshd first-match
precedence, Match-all scope resumption, ChallengeResponseAuthentication
aliasing, exempt roster/ceiling, prohibit-password/without-password
acceptance, ledger schema migration, and compliant-host idempotency.

The 60-case randomized test exercises all amended clauses in combination
against a reference implementation that the agent cannot access, making it
infeasible to pass without correctly implementing the resolved policy. The
agent has no path to extract expected outputs since tests/ is mounted only
in the verifier container, and randomized inputs prevent fixture-only
hardcoding.

================================================================================

✅ pass - informative_test_structure: tests/test_outputs.py is clearly organized: each test function has a descriptive name and a docstring explaining exactly what it checks. Tests are grouped logically (health, random policy, specific fixture tests, ledger test, compliant baseline). helpers.py clearly separates HTTP client helpers, the reference implementation, and the snapshot generator. test.sh provides clear pre-test build and server-startup steps with informative error messages.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The reference policy implementation (expected_patches/normalize/etc.) lives only in tests/helpers.py, which is not copied into the image (Dockerfile copies cpp-auditor/, fixtures/, and docs/ — not tests/ or solution/). The agent cannot simply copy or read the reference Python implementation. The agent must derive the resolved policy from the 300KB standard.md (a long-context challenge), which embeds normative controls among realistic filler. The test.sh resets the ledger at verifier run time, preventing the agent from pre-baking a ledger. The solution is in solution/ which is also not copied into the image.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: instruction.md explicitly specifies the API endpoint (POST /v1/setup/audit), the request schema ({"files": {...}}), the response schema ({"patches": [...]}), and enumerates all three patch action types with their fields: sudoers.require_password (target), ssh.revoke_keys (target), and systemd.set_dropin (unit/key/value). This is normative, not just illustrative.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: requirements.txt uses --require-hashes with exact version pins and sha256 hashes for all Python dependencies (iniconfig, packaging, pluggy, pytest, pytest-json-ctrf). ✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solution/solve.sh copies corrected source files (parse.cpp, audit.cpp, main.cpp) over the buggy originals, then runs a real CMake build. [Container] 2026/06/26 05:14:38.635315 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found; task may use steps/*/instruction.md'
Our lab access gateway runs a C++ setup auditor in `/app/cpp-auditor`. It takes a
snapshot of a host's configuration files (passwd, shadow, group, sudoers,
authorized_keys, and the sshd drop-ins) and proposes the patches needed to bring
the host into policy. It exposes `POST /v1/setup/audit` with a request body of
the form `{"files": {...}}` and replies with `{"patches": [...]}`, where each
patch is one of `sudoers.require_password` (target), `ssh.revoke_keys` (target),
or `systemd.set_dropin` (unit/key/value).

The authoritative policy is the **Lab Access Hardening Standard** in
`/app/docs/standard.md`. The auditor has fallen behind the Standard: it predates
several changes and also has a few outright parsing defects, so it now disagrees
with policy on many hosts.

The auditor's logic lives mainly in `/app/cpp-auditor/src/parse.cpp` (turns the
raw files into a normalized inventory) and `/app/cpp-auditor/src/audit.cpp`
(turns that inventory into patches). Fix the service so that, for any host
snapshot, `POST /v1/setup/audit` returns exactly the patch set the Standard
implies; a host that is already compliant must produce an empty patch set.
Rebuild with the provided CMake setup (the existing `build/` target) after
editing.

The auditor must also be **stateless**: the patch set returned for a request must
be a pure function of the snapshot in that request, unaffected by any snapshot
audited earlier in the same run. Verification audits many different hosts against
a single long-lived server, so anything that lets one host's data carry over to
another will show up as incorrect patches.

Finally, the auditor keeps a persistent **audit ledger** at
`/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json` and is supposed to append every patch it
emits. Operators have noticed the ledger is no longer being updated even though
audits keep returning results, so its record has gone stale. Restore durable
logging so that each audit's patches are appended to the ledger and earlier
records are preserved; investigate the file on disk to understand why the current
build stopped writing to it.

The behaviors the auditor must get right, all governed by the Standard, are:

- **disabled-account detection** — which `shadow` password tokens and which
  non-interactive login shells mark an account disabled;
- **effective group membership** — including members who belong via their
  *primary* group id, not just the textual member list;
- **passwordless-sudo determination** — which `sudoers` rules actually grant it
  (the scope `NOPASSWD` applies to, alias/`%group`/negation resolution, and
  per-user/group `Defaults` overrides);
- **service-account exemptions** — the exempt roster and the user-id ceiling;
- **effective sshd drop-in settings** — the fragment precedence rules and which
  keywords and values are accepted (including the keyboard-interactive keyword
  and its deprecated alias).

Read the Standard carefully. It is maintained as a body of numbered controls
followed by an **authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G)**; where the body
and an amendment conflict, the amendment governs (see section 1.4), so several
controls do not mean quite what their body text says on its own. The exact token
sets, accepted values, ceilings, and overrides are defined there as amended.

The toolchain (cmake, g++) is already installed and everything runs offline.

                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ✅ PASS
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires fixing a C++ security auditor that parses host configuration
snapshots (passwd, shadow, group, sudoers, sshd drop-ins) and proposes
remediation patches according to a detailed Lab Access Hardening Standard. The
agent must correct parsing defects, update policy logic to match amended
controls (Appendix G), restore per-request isolation, and fix a broken audit
ledger. The test suite validates correctness with 12 deterministic fixture
tests plus 60 randomized host snapshots, covering disabled-account detection,
group membership, sudoers semantics, sshd precedence rules, and ledger
migration.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Pytest Version Mismatch Between requirements.txt and Skill Example
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/requirements.txt (line 16)
Problem: The requirements.txt pins pytest==8.2.2, which is slightly older than
         the recommended 8.4.1. This is not a functional issue since versions
         are pinned with hashes, but newer pytest versions may offer better
         diagnostics and CTRF compatibility.

Current approach: pytest==8.2.2 with full hash pins

Suggested fix: Consider upgrading to pytest==8.4.1 in a future update, though
the current pin is functional and fully reproducible.

Explanation: Minor version difference; the task works correctly as-is. The hash
pinning ensures reproducibility regardless of version choice.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Add --ctrf Plugin Availability Check in test.sh
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test.sh (line 45)

Current approach: test.sh invokes pytest with `--ctrf /logs/verifier/ctrf.json`
which requires the pytest-json-ctrf plugin. The plugin IS installed via
requirements.txt, but if it ever fails to load, the entire test run would fail
rather than gracefully degrading.

Suggested improvement:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  PYTHONPATH=/tests python3 -m pytest -p no:cacheprovider -rA               │
│      --ctrf /logs/verifier/ctrf.json /tests/test_outputs.py -q || true     │
│  # Fallback: re-run without --ctrf if plugin missing                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: This is a minor robustness suggestion. The current setup works since
pytest-json-ctrf is correctly installed in requirements.txt with hash pins.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is an excellent, well-crafted Terminal-Bench task that tests deep code
comprehension across C++ parsing logic, security policy interpretation, and
stateful service debugging. The environment is properly configured with all
dependencies installed at build time, the solution is non-trivial, and the
test suite provides thorough coverage through both deterministic fixtures and
randomized property-based testing.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Exceptional task design — multi-layered debugging across parsing, policy,
    statefulness, and persistence with a rich authoritative document to reason
    about
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating: reference implementation lives only in test helpers
    (hidden from agent); environment code is intentionally buggy, not a copy
    of the solution
  ✓ Comprehensive test coverage with 12 targeted fixture tests plus 60
    randomized snapshots exercising every amended policy clause

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ None significant — task is well-structured and production-quality

Evaluates: C++ debugging, security policy interpretation, long-context
           document comprehension, stateful service architecture

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ✅ READY TO USE

  This task is exceptionally well-designed with proper offline setup, pinned
  dependencies, thorough tests, and a challenging multi-faceted debugging
  problem. Ready for deployment without modifications.
================================================================================
