[Container] 2026/06/26 05:14:41.682041 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found'
Our lab access gateway runs a C++ setup auditor in `/app/cpp-auditor`. It takes a
snapshot of a host's configuration files (passwd, shadow, group, sudoers,
authorized_keys, and the sshd drop-ins) and proposes the patches needed to bring
the host into policy. It exposes `POST /v1/setup/audit` with a request body of
the form `{"files": {...}}` and replies with `{"patches": [...]}`, where each
patch is one of `sudoers.require_password` (target), `ssh.revoke_keys` (target),
or `systemd.set_dropin` (unit/key/value).

The authoritative policy is the **Lab Access Hardening Standard** in
`/app/docs/standard.md`. The auditor has fallen behind the Standard: it predates
several changes and also has a few outright parsing defects, so it now disagrees
with policy on many hosts.

The auditor's logic lives mainly in `/app/cpp-auditor/src/parse.cpp` (turns the
raw files into a normalized inventory) and `/app/cpp-auditor/src/audit.cpp`
(turns that inventory into patches). Fix the service so that, for any host
snapshot, `POST /v1/setup/audit` returns exactly the patch set the Standard
implies; a host that is already compliant must produce an empty patch set.
Rebuild with the provided CMake setup (the existing `build/` target) after
editing.

The auditor must also be **stateless**: the patch set returned for a request must
be a pure function of the snapshot in that request, unaffected by any snapshot
audited earlier in the same run. Verification audits many different hosts against
a single long-lived server, so anything that lets one host's data carry over to
another will show up as incorrect patches.

Finally, the auditor keeps a persistent **audit ledger** at
`/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json` and is supposed to append every patch it
emits. Operators have noticed the ledger is no longer being updated even though
audits keep returning results, so its record has gone stale. Restore durable
logging so that each audit's patches are appended to the ledger and earlier
records are preserved; investigate the file on disk to understand why the current
build stopped writing to it.

The behaviors the auditor must get right, all governed by the Standard, are:

- **disabled-account detection** — which `shadow` password tokens and which
  non-interactive login shells mark an account disabled;
- **effective group membership** — including members who belong via their
  *primary* group id, not just the textual member list;
- **passwordless-sudo determination** — which `sudoers` rules actually grant it
  (the scope `NOPASSWD` applies to, alias/`%group`/negation resolution, and
  per-user/group `Defaults` overrides);
- **service-account exemptions** — the exempt roster and the user-id ceiling;
- **effective sshd drop-in settings** — the fragment precedence rules and which
  keywords and values are accepted (including the keyboard-interactive keyword
  and its deprecated alias).

Read the Standard carefully. It is maintained as a body of numbered controls
followed by an **authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G)**; where the body
and an amendment conflict, the amendment governs (see section 1.4), so several
controls do not mean quite what their body text says on its own. The exact token
sets, accepted values, ceilings, and overrides are defined there as amended.

The toolchain (cmake, g++) is already installed and everything runs offline.

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite provides comprehensive coverage of all core constraints through
targeted fixture tests, a 60-scenario randomized property check against a
faithful reference implementation, and implicit verification of statefulness
requirements.

Strengths:  The combination of deterministic fixture tests for each amended
control and a randomized property-based test against a hidden reference
implementation makes shortcutting effectively impossible. The reference
implementation in helpers.py faithfully encodes every clause of the standard
as amended by Appendix G.

Weaknesses: The statefulness requirement is tested only implicitly through
the randomized test running 60 requests against one server instance; a
dedicated test that explicitly verifies cross-request isolation would make
failures more diagnosable.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite verifies 14 distinct scenarios across all instruction
constraints: disabled-account detection (locked tokens, extended shells),
primary-group membership, NOPASSWD:ALL narrowing, Defaults:!authenticate
grants, last-match-wins semantics, includedir splicing, sshd first-match
precedence, Match-all scope resumption, ChallengeResponseAuthentication
aliasing, exempt roster/ceiling, prohibit-password/without-password
acceptance, ledger schema migration, and compliant-host idempotency.

The 60-case randomized test exercises all amended clauses in combination
against a reference implementation that the agent cannot access, making it
infeasible to pass without correctly implementing the resolved policy. The
agent has no path to extract expected outputs since tests/ is mounted only
in the verifier container, and randomized inputs prevent fixture-only
hardcoding.

================================================================================

[Container] 2026/06/26 05:14:41.682041 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found'
Our lab access gateway runs a C++ setup auditor in `/app/cpp-auditor`. It takes a
snapshot of a host's configuration files (passwd, shadow, group, sudoers,
authorized_keys, and the sshd drop-ins) and proposes the patches needed to bring
the host into policy. It exposes `POST /v1/setup/audit` with a request body of
the form `{"files": {...}}` and replies with `{"patches": [...]}`, where each
patch is one of `sudoers.require_password` (target), `ssh.revoke_keys` (target),
or `systemd.set_dropin` (unit/key/value).

The authoritative policy is the **Lab Access Hardening Standard** in
`/app/docs/standard.md`. The auditor has fallen behind the Standard: it predates
several changes and also has a few outright parsing defects, so it now disagrees
with policy on many hosts.

The auditor's logic lives mainly in `/app/cpp-auditor/src/parse.cpp` (turns the
raw files into a normalized inventory) and `/app/cpp-auditor/src/audit.cpp`
(turns that inventory into patches). Fix the service so that, for any host
snapshot, `POST /v1/setup/audit` returns exactly the patch set the Standard
implies; a host that is already compliant must produce an empty patch set.
Rebuild with the provided CMake setup (the existing `build/` target) after
editing.

The auditor must also be **stateless**: the patch set returned for a request must
be a pure function of the snapshot in that request, unaffected by any snapshot
audited earlier in the same run. Verification audits many different hosts against
a single long-lived server, so anything that lets one host's data carry over to
another will show up as incorrect patches.

Finally, the auditor keeps a persistent **audit ledger** at
`/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json` and is supposed to append every patch it
emits. Operators have noticed the ledger is no longer being updated even though
audits keep returning results, so its record has gone stale. Restore durable
logging so that each audit's patches are appended to the ledger and earlier
records are preserved; investigate the file on disk to understand why the current
build stopped writing to it.

The behaviors the auditor must get right, all governed by the Standard, are:

- **disabled-account detection** — which `shadow` password tokens and which
  non-interactive login shells mark an account disabled;
- **effective group membership** — including members who belong via their
  *primary* group id, not just the textual member list;
- **passwordless-sudo determination** — which `sudoers` rules actually grant it
  (the scope `NOPASSWD` applies to, alias/`%group`/negation resolution, and
  per-user/group `Defaults` overrides);
- **service-account exemptions** — the exempt roster and the user-id ceiling;
- **effective sshd drop-in settings** — the fragment precedence rules and which
  keywords and values are accepted (including the keyboard-interactive keyword
  and its deprecated alias).

Read the Standard carefully. It is maintained as a body of numbered controls
followed by an **authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G)**; where the body
and an amendment conflict, the amendment governs (see section 1.4), so several
controls do not mean quite what their body text says on its own. The exact token
sets, accepted values, ceilings, and overrides are defined there as amended.

The toolchain (cmake, g++) is already installed and everything runs offline.

## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: instruction.md explicitly describes all behaviors tested: the five major policy areas (disabled-account detection, effective group membership, passwordless-sudo determination, service-account exemptions, effective sshd drop-in settings), the statelessness requirement, the audit ledger migration/append requirement, the API endpoint and request/response schema, and the precedence rule (Appendix G amendments supersede the body). Each test in test_outputs.py maps to one of these stated behaviors.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: The tests cover all the behavior described in instruction.md: disabled-account detection (test_locked_and_extended_shells_lose_keys), effective group membership (test_primary_group_membership_grants_sudo), passwordless sudo with NOPASSWD:ALL, negation, aliases, Defaults !authenticate, last-match, and includedir (test_nopasswd_all_and_negation, test_sudo_defaults_authenticate_grants_passwordless, test_sudo_last_match_wins, test_sudo_includedir_splices_in_order), service-account exemptions (test_exempt_ceiling_and_roster), sshd precedence with all three keywords including alias (test_sshd_precedence_and_prohibit_password, test_sshd_kbdinteractive_alias_first_match, test_sshd_match_all_resumes_global), statelessness (exercised via random host testing with a single long-lived server), ledger migration and append (test_audit_ledger_migrated_and_records_patches), compliant host yields empty set (test_compliant_host_needs_no_patches), and broad randomized coverage (test_matches_resolved_policy_on_random_hosts).
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: tests/test_outputs.py is clearly organized: each test function has a descriptive name and a docstring explaining exactly what it checks. Tests are grouped logically (health, random policy, specific fixture tests, ledger test, compliant baseline). helpers.py clearly separates HTTP client helpers, the reference implementation, and the snapshot generator. test.sh provides clear pre-test build and server-startup steps with informative error messages.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The reference policy implementation (expected_patches/normalize/etc.) lives only in tests/helpers.py, which is not copied into the image (Dockerfile copies cpp-auditor/, fixtures/, and docs/ — not tests/ or solution/). The agent cannot simply copy or read the reference Python implementation. The agent must derive the resolved policy from the 300KB standard.md (a long-context challenge), which embeds normative controls among realistic filler. The test.sh resets the ledger at verifier run time, preventing the agent from pre-baking a ledger. The solution is in solution/ which is also not copied into the image.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: instruction.md explicitly specifies the API endpoint (POST /v1/setup/audit), the request schema ({"files": {...}}), the response schema ({"patches": [...]}), and enumerates all three patch action types with their fields: sudoers.require_password (target), ssh.revoke_keys (target), and systemd.set_dropin (unit/key/value). This is normative, not just illustrative.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: requirements.txt uses --require-hashes with exact version pins and sha256 hashes for all Python dependencies (iniconfig, packaging, pluggy, pytest, pytest-json-ctrf). The Dockerfile pins apt packages with exact version strings (g++=4:12.2.0-3, cmake=3.25.1-1, make=4.3-4.1, nlohmann-json3-dev=3.11.2-2, etc.). The base image is pinned by SHA256 digest. The cpp-httplib header is vendored in-tree (no external fetch at runtime).
✅ pass - typos: No typos were found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names across any of the files examined. All file paths in the Dockerfile, test scripts, and instruction.md are consistent and correct.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only COPYs cpp-auditor/, fixtures/, and docs/ into the image. Neither tests/ nor solution/ are copied in. The .dockerignore does not include tests/ or solution/ (they are simply never COPY'd). The test.sh mounts /tests from outside the image at run time (PYTHONPATH=/tests).
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solution/solve.sh copies corrected source files (parse.cpp, audit.cpp, main.cpp) over the buggy originals, then runs a real CMake build. The solution files contain genuine C++ implementations that derive results by parsing input data—not by echoing pre-computed answers. The logic is substantive and mirrors the policy rules described in the standard.
➖ not_applicable - file_reference_mentioned: This task does not require the agent to produce output files; it requires fixing a C++ service. The tests communicate with the running service over HTTP. No output file needs to be named in instruction.md.

The agent must derive the resolved policy from the 300KB standard.md (a long-context challenge), which embeds normative controls among realistic filler. The test.sh resets the ledger at verifier run time, preventing the agent from pre-baking a ledger. The solution is in solution/ which is also not copied into the image.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: instruction.md explicitly specifies the API endpoint (POST /v1/setup/audit), the request schema ({"files": {...}}), the response schema ({"patches": [...]}), and enumerates all three patch action types with their fields: sudoers.require_password (target), ssh.revoke_keys (target), and systemd.set_dropin (unit/key/value). This is normative, not just illustrative.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: requirements.txt uses --require-hashes with exact version pins and sha256 hashes for all Python dependencies (iniconfig, packaging, pluggy, pytest, pytest-json-ctrf). The Dockerfile pins apt packages with exact version strings (g++=4:12.2.0-3, cmake=3.25.1-1, make=4.3-4.1, nlohmann-json3-dev=3.11.2-2, etc.). The base image is pinned by SHA256 digest. The cpp-httplib header is vendored in-tree (no external fetch at runtime).
✅ pass - typos: No typos were found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names across any of the files examined. All file paths in the Dockerfile, test scripts, and instruction.md are consistent and correct.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only COPYs cpp-auditor/, fixtures/, and docs/ into the image. Neither tests/ nor solution/ are copied in. The .dockerignore does not include tests/ or solution/ (they are simply never COPY'd). The test.sh mounts /tests from outside the image at run time (PYTHONPATH=/tests).
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solution/solve.sh copies corrected source files (parse.cpp, audit.cpp, main.cpp) over the buggy originals, then runs a real CMake build. The solution files contain genuine C++ implementations that derive results by parsing input data—not by echoing pre-computed answers. The logic is substantive and mirrors the policy rules described in the standard.
➖ not_applicable - file_reference_mentioned: This task does not require the agent to produce output files; it requires fixing a C++ service. The tests communicate with the running service over HTTP. No output file needs to be named in instruction.md.

[Container] 2026/06/26 05:14:38.635315 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found; task may use steps/*/instruction.md'
Our lab access gateway runs a C++ setup auditor in `/app/cpp-auditor`. It takes a
snapshot of a host's configuration files (passwd, shadow, group, sudoers,
authorized_keys, and the sshd drop-ins) and proposes the patches needed to bring
the host into policy. It exposes `POST /v1/setup/audit` with a request body of
the form `{"files": {...}}` and replies with `{"patches": [...]}`, where each
patch is one of `sudoers.require_password` (target), `ssh.revoke_keys` (target),
or `systemd.set_dropin` (unit/key/value).

The authoritative policy is the **Lab Access Hardening Standard** in
`/app/docs/standard.md`. The auditor has fallen behind the Standard: it predates
several changes and also has a few outright parsing defects, so it now disagrees
with policy on many hosts.

The auditor's logic lives mainly in `/app/cpp-auditor/src/parse.cpp` (turns the
raw files into a normalized inventory) and `/app/cpp-auditor/src/audit.cpp`
(turns that inventory into patches). Fix the service so that, for any host
snapshot, `POST /v1/setup/audit` returns exactly the patch set the Standard
implies; a host that is already compliant must produce an empty patch set.
Rebuild with the provided CMake setup (the existing `build/` target) after
editing.

The auditor must also be **stateless**: the patch set returned for a request must
be a pure function of the snapshot in that request, unaffected by any snapshot
audited earlier in the same run. Verification audits many different hosts against
a single long-lived server, so anything that lets one host's data carry over to
another will show up as incorrect patches.

Finally, the auditor keeps a persistent **audit ledger** at
`/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json` and is supposed to append every patch it
emits. Operators have noticed the ledger is no longer being updated even though
audits keep returning results, so its record has gone stale. Restore durable
logging so that each audit's patches are appended to the ledger and earlier
records are preserved; investigate the file on disk to understand why the current
build stopped writing to it.

The behaviors the auditor must get right, all governed by the Standard, are:

- **disabled-account detection** — which `shadow` password tokens and which
  non-interactive login shells mark an account disabled;
- **effective group membership** — including members who belong via their
  *primary* group id, not just the textual member list;
- **passwordless-sudo determination** — which `sudoers` rules actually grant it
  (the scope `NOPASSWD` applies to, alias/`%group`/negation resolution, and
  per-user/group `Defaults` overrides);
- **service-account exemptions** — the exempt roster and the user-id ceiling;
- **effective sshd drop-in settings** — the fragment precedence rules and which
  keywords and values are accepted (including the keyboard-interactive keyword
  and its deprecated alias).

Read the Standard carefully. It is maintained as a body of numbered controls
followed by an **authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G)**; where the body
and an amendment conflict, the amendment governs (see section 1.4), so several
controls do not mean quite what their body text says on its own. The exact token
sets, accepted values, ceilings, and overrides are defined there as amended.

The toolchain (cmake, g++) is already installed and everything runs offline.

# Copy results back and upload to S3
cat output/message.json
aws s3 cp output/message.json ${OUTPUT_S3_URI}

# Rubric 1
Agent reads the Standard document (docs/standard.md) including Appendix G amendments before making code changes, +2
Agent extends the non-interactive shell set to include /usr/bin/git-shell and /bin/sync per G-2026-01, +3
Agent adds *LK* to the locked-password token set in is_disabled per G-2026-01, +2
Agent implements primary-GID membership in effective_group_members (accounts whose gid matches the group's gid are members), +3
Agent narrows NOPASSWD detection to require NOPASSWD applied to ALL (not just presence of NOPASSWD substring) per G-2026-02, +3
Agent implements Defaults:<binder> !authenticate as granting passwordless sudo per G-2026-05, +3
Agent implements last-match-wins semantics for sudoers passwordless determination per G-2026-07, +3
Agent implements #includedir/@includedir resolution splicing sudoers.d files in sorted order per G-2026-09, +3
Agent implements sshd Match block handling: non-all Match suspends evaluation, Match all resumes global scope per G-2026-08, +3
Agent folds ChallengeResponseAuthentication as deprecated alias of KbdInteractiveAuthentication per G-2026-06, +2
Agent breaks the existing code or introduces compilation errors that are never resolved, -5

# Rubric 2
Agent lowers the service-account UID ceiling from 999 to 499 in audit.cpp per G-2026-03, +3
Agent extends the exempt-account roster to include svc_backup per G-2026-03, +2
Agent adds KbdInteractiveAuthentication to the list of evaluated sshd keywords per HD-SSHD-KBDINT, +3
Agent accepts prohibit-password as valid for PermitRootLogin per G-2026-04, +2
Agent accepts without-password as deprecated spelling of prohibit-password for PermitRootLogin per G-2026-10, +2
Agent ensures sshd_value_accepted is key-aware (prohibit-password/without-password only for PermitRootLogin, not other keys), +2
Agent modifies audit rules without understanding the Standard, producing incorrect patch logic, -3

# Rubric 3
Agent identifies and removes the static g_registry global that causes cross-request state contamination, +5
Agent ensures each POST /v1/setup/audit request uses only the snapshot from that request with no carried-over account data, +3
Agent identifies that the ledger file uses legacy schema 1 with audits key while code expects entries, +2
Agent fixes the ledger to migrate from schema 1 (audits) to schema 2 (entries) while preserving existing records, +3
Agent ensures the ledger appends new patches on each audit without silently swallowing write errors, +2
Agent successfully rebuilds the project with CMake after all edits, +3
Agent runs the rebuilt server and tests it with at least one POST request to verify behavior, +2
Agent accidentally deletes or corrupts the existing ledger records during migration, -2

                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ✅ PASS
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires fixing a C++ security auditor that parses host configuration
snapshots (passwd, shadow, group, sudoers, sshd drop-ins) and proposes
remediation patches according to a detailed Lab Access Hardening Standard. The
agent must correct parsing defects, update policy logic to match amended
controls (Appendix G), restore per-request isolation, and fix a broken audit
ledger. The test suite validates correctness with 12 deterministic fixture
tests plus 60 randomized host snapshots, covering disabled-account detection,
group membership, sudoers semantics, sshd precedence rules, and ledger
migration.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Pytest Version Mismatch Between requirements.txt and Skill Example
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/requirements.txt (line 16)
Problem: The requirements.txt pins pytest==8.2.2, which is slightly older than
         the recommended 8.4.1. This is not a functional issue since versions
         are pinned with hashes, but newer pytest versions may offer better
         diagnostics and CTRF compatibility.

Current approach: pytest==8.2.2 with full hash pins

Suggested fix: Consider upgrading to pytest==8.4.1 in a future update, though
the current pin is functional and fully reproducible.

Explanation: Minor version difference; the task works correctly as-is. The hash
pinning ensures reproducibility regardless of version choice.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Add --ctrf Plugin Availability Check in test.sh
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test.sh (line 45)

Current approach: test.sh invokes pytest with `--ctrf /logs/verifier/ctrf.json`
which requires the pytest-json-ctrf plugin. The plugin IS installed via
requirements.txt, but if it ever fails to load, the entire test run would fail
rather than gracefully degrading.

Suggested improvement:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  PYTHONPATH=/tests python3 -m pytest -p no:cacheprovider -rA               │
│      --ctrf /logs/verifier/ctrf.json /tests/test_outputs.py -q || true     │
│  # Fallback: re-run without --ctrf if plugin missing                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: This is a minor robustness suggestion. The current setup works since
pytest-json-ctrf is correctly installed in requirements.txt with hash pins.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is an excellent, well-crafted Terminal-Bench task that tests deep code
comprehension across C++ parsing logic, security policy interpretation, and
stateful service debugging. The environment is properly configured with all
dependencies installed at build time, the solution is non-trivial, and the
test suite provides thorough coverage through both deterministic fixtures and
randomized property-based testing.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Exceptional task design — multi-layered debugging across parsing, policy,
    statefulness, and persistence with a rich authoritative document to reason
    about
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating: reference implementation lives only in test helpers
    (hidden from agent); environment code is intentionally buggy, not a copy
    of the solution
  ✓ Comprehensive test coverage with 12 targeted fixture tests plus 60
    randomized snapshots exercising every amended policy clause

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ None significant — task is well-structured and production-quality

Evaluates: C++ debugging, security policy interpretation, long-context
           document comprehension, stateful service architecture

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ✅ READY TO USE

  This task is exceptionally well-designed with proper offline setup, pinned
  dependencies, thorough tests, and a challenging multi-faceted debugging
  problem. Ready for deployment without modifications.

================================================================================
                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ✅ PASS
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires fixing a C++ security auditor that parses host configuration
snapshots (passwd, shadow, group, sudoers, sshd drop-ins) and proposes
remediation patches according to a detailed Lab Access Hardening Standard. The
agent must correct parsing defects, update policy logic to match amended
controls (Appendix G), restore per-request isolation, and fix a broken audit
ledger. The test suite validates correctness with 12 deterministic fixture
tests plus 60 randomized host snapshots, covering disabled-account detection,
group membership, sudoers semantics, sshd precedence rules, and ledger
migration.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Pytest Version Mismatch Between requirements.txt and Skill Example
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/requirements.txt (line 16)
Problem: The requirements.txt pins pytest==8.2.2, which is slightly older than
         the recommended 8.4.1. This is not a functional issue since versions
         are pinned with hashes, but newer pytest versions may offer better
         diagnostics and CTRF compatibility.

Current approach: pytest==8.2.2 with full hash pins

Suggested fix: Consider upgrading to pytest==8.4.1 in a future update, though
the current pin is functional and fully reproducible.

Explanation: Minor version difference; the task works correctly as-is. The hash
pinning ensures reproducibility regardless of version choice.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Add --ctrf Plugin Availability Check in test.sh
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test.sh (line 45)

Current approach: test.sh invokes pytest with `--ctrf /logs/verifier/ctrf.json`
which requires the pytest-json-ctrf plugin. The plugin IS installed via
requirements.txt, but if it ever fails to load, the entire test run would fail
rather than gracefully degrading.

Suggested improvement:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  PYTHONPATH=/tests python3 -m pytest -p no:cacheprovider -rA               │
│      --ctrf /logs/verifier/ctrf.json /tests/test_outputs.py -q || true     │
│  # Fallback: re-run without --ctrf if plugin missing                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: This is a minor robustness suggestion. The current setup works since
pytest-json-ctrf is correctly installed in requirements.txt with hash pins.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is an excellent, well-crafted Terminal-Bench task that tests deep code
comprehension across C++ parsing logic, security policy interpretation, and
stateful service debugging. The environment is properly configured with all
dependencies installed at build time, the solution is non-trivial, and the
test suite provides thorough coverage through both deterministic fixtures and
randomized property-based testing.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Exceptional task design — multi-layered debugging across parsing, policy,
    statefulness, and persistence with a rich authoritative document to reason
    about
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating: reference implementation lives only in test helpers
    (hidden from agent); environment code is intentionally buggy, not a copy
    of the solution
  ✓ Comprehensive test coverage with 12 targeted fixture tests plus 60
    randomized snapshots exercising every amended policy clause

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ None significant — task is well-structured and production-quality

Evaluates: C++ debugging, security policy interpretation, long-context
           document comprehension, stateful service architecture

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ✅ READY TO USE

  This task is exceptionally well-designed with proper offline setup, pinned
  dependencies, thorough tests, and a challenging multi-faceted debugging
  problem. Ready for deployment without modifications.
================================================================================

# Rubric 1
Agent reads the Standard document (docs/standard.md) including Appendix G amendments before making code changes, +2
Agent extends the non-interactive shell set to include /usr/bin/git-shell and /bin/sync per G-2026-01, +3
Agent adds *LK* to the locked-password token set in is_disabled per G-2026-01, +2
Agent implements primary-GID membership in effective_group_members (accounts whose gid matches the group's gid are members), +3
Agent narrows NOPASSWD detection to require NOPASSWD applied to ALL (not just presence of NOPASSWD substring) per G-2026-02, +3
Agent implements Defaults:<binder> !authenticate as granting passwordless sudo per G-2026-05, +3
Agent implements last-match-wins semantics for sudoers passwordless determination per G-2026-07, +3
Agent implements #includedir/@includedir resolution splicing sudoers.d files in sorted order per G-2026-09, +3
Agent implements sshd Match block handling: non-all Match suspends evaluation, Match all resumes global scope per G-2026-08, +3
Agent folds ChallengeResponseAuthentication as deprecated alias of KbdInteractiveAuthentication per G-2026-06, +2
Agent breaks the existing code or introduces compilation errors that are never resolved, -5

# Rubric 2
Agent lowers the service-account UID ceiling from 999 to 499 in audit.cpp per G-2026-03, +3
Agent extends the exempt-account roster to include svc_backup per G-2026-03, +2
Agent adds KbdInteractiveAuthentication to the list of evaluated sshd keywords per HD-SSHD-KBDINT, +3
Agent accepts prohibit-password as valid for PermitRootLogin per G-2026-04, +2
Agent accepts without-password as deprecated spelling of prohibit-password for PermitRootLogin per G-2026-10, +2
Agent ensures sshd_value_accepted is key-aware (prohibit-password/without-password only for PermitRootLogin, not other keys), +2
Agent modifies audit rules without understanding the Standard, producing incorrect patch logic, -3

# Rubric 3
Agent identifies and removes the static g_registry global that causes cross-request state contamination, +5
Agent ensures each POST /v1/setup/audit request uses only the snapshot from that request with no carried-over account data, +3
Agent identifies that the ledger file uses legacy schema 1 with audits key while code expects entries, +2
Agent fixes the ledger to migrate from schema 1 (audits) to schema 2 (entries) while preserving existing records, +3
Agent ensures the ledger appends new patches on each audit without silently swallowing write errors, +2
Agent successfully rebuilds the project with CMake after all edits, +3
Agent runs the rebuilt server and tests it with at least one POST request to verify behavior, +2
Agent accidentally deletes or corrupts the existing ledger records during migration, -2

[Container] 2026/06/26 05:14:38.635315 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found; task may use steps/*/instruction.md'
Our lab access gateway runs a C++ setup auditor in `/app/cpp-auditor`. It takes a
snapshot of a host's configuration files (passwd, shadow, group, sudoers,
authorized_keys, and the sshd drop-ins) and proposes the patches needed to bring
the host into policy. It exposes `POST /v1/setup/audit` with a request body of
the form `{"files": {...}}` and replies with `{"patches": [...]}`, where each
patch is one of `sudoers.require_password` (target), `ssh.revoke_keys` (target),
or `systemd.set_dropin` (unit/key/value).

The authoritative policy is the **Lab Access Hardening Standard** in
`/app/docs/standard.md`. The auditor has fallen behind the Standard: it predates
several changes and also has a few outright parsing defects, so it now disagrees
with policy on many hosts.

The auditor's logic lives mainly in `/app/cpp-auditor/src/parse.cpp` (turns the
raw files into a normalized inventory) and `/app/cpp-auditor/src/audit.cpp`
(turns that inventory into patches). Fix the service so that, for any host
snapshot, `POST /v1/setup/audit` returns exactly the patch set the Standard
implies; a host that is already compliant must produce an empty patch set.
Rebuild with the provided CMake setup (the existing `build/` target) after
editing.

The auditor must also be **stateless**: the patch set returned for a request must
be a pure function of the snapshot in that request, unaffected by any snapshot
audited earlier in the same run. Verification audits many different hosts against
a single long-lived server, so anything that lets one host's data carry over to
another will show up as incorrect patches.

Finally, the auditor keeps a persistent **audit ledger** at
`/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json` and is supposed to append every patch it
emits. Operators have noticed the ledger is no longer being updated even though
audits keep returning results, so its record has gone stale. Restore durable
logging so that each audit's patches are appended to the ledger and earlier
records are preserved; investigate the file on disk to understand why the current
build stopped writing to it.

The behaviors the auditor must get right, all governed by the Standard, are:

- **disabled-account detection** — which `shadow` password tokens and which
  non-interactive login shells mark an account disabled;
- **effective group membership** — including members who belong via their
  *primary* group id, not just the textual member list;
- **passwordless-sudo determination** — which `sudoers` rules actually grant it
  (the scope `NOPASSWD` applies to, alias/`%group`/negation resolution, and
  per-user/group `Defaults` overrides);
- **service-account exemptions** — the exempt roster and the user-id ceiling;
- **effective sshd drop-in settings** — the fragment precedence rules and which
  keywords and values are accepted (including the keyboard-interactive keyword
  and its deprecated alias).

Read the Standard carefully. It is maintained as a body of numbered controls
followed by an **authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G)**; where the body
and an amendment conflict, the amendment governs (see section 1.4), so several
controls do not mean quite what their body text says on its own. The exact token
sets, accepted values, ceilings, and overrides are defined there as amended.

The toolchain (cmake, g++) is already installed and everything runs offline.

                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task

Status:        ✅ PASS
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires fixing a C++ security auditor that parses host configuration
snapshots (passwd, shadow, group, sudoers, sshd drop-ins) and proposes
remediation patches according to a detailed Lab Access Hardening Standard. The
agent must correct parsing defects, update policy logic to match amended
controls (Appendix G), restore per-request isolation, and fix a broken audit
ledger. The test suite validates correctness with 12 deterministic fixture
tests plus 60 randomized host snapshots, covering disabled-account detection,
group membership, sudoers semantics, sshd precedence rules, and ledger
migration.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Pytest Version Mismatch Between requirements.txt and Skill Example
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/requirements.txt (line 16)
Problem: The requirements.txt pins pytest==8.2.2, which is slightly older than
         the recommended 8.4.1. This is not a functional issue since versions
         are pinned with hashes, but newer pytest versions may offer better
         diagnostics and CTRF compatibility.

Current approach: pytest==8.2.2 with full hash pins

Suggested fix: Consider upgrading to pytest==8.4.1 in a future update, though
the current pin is functional and fully reproducible.

Explanation: Minor version difference; the task works correctly as-is. The hash
pinning ensures reproducibility regardless of version choice.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Add --ctrf Plugin Availability Check in test.sh
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test.sh (line 45)

Current approach: test.sh invokes pytest with `--ctrf /logs/verifier/ctrf.json`
which requires the pytest-json-ctrf plugin. The plugin IS installed via
requirements.txt, but if it ever fails to load, the entire test run would fail
rather than gracefully degrading.

Suggested improvement:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  PYTHONPATH=/tests python3 -m pytest -p no:cacheprovider -rA               │
│      --ctrf /logs/verifier/ctrf.json /tests/test_outputs.py -q || true     │
│  # Fallback: re-run without --ctrf if plugin missing                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: This is a minor robustness suggestion. The current setup works since
pytest-json-ctrf is correctly installed in requirements.txt with hash pins.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is an excellent, well-crafted Terminal-Bench task that tests deep code
comprehension across C++ parsing logic, security policy interpretation, and
stateful service debugging. The environment is properly configured with all
dependencies installed at build time, the solution is non-trivial, and the
test suite provides thorough coverage through both deterministic fixtures and
randomized property-based testing.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Exceptional task design — multi-layered debugging across parsing, policy,
    statefulness, and persistence with a rich authoritative document to reason
    about
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating: reference implementation lives only in test helpers
    (hidden from agent); environment code is intentionally buggy, not a copy
    of the solution
  ✓ Comprehensive test coverage with 12 targeted fixture tests plus 60
    randomized snapshots exercising every amended policy clause

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ None significant — task is well-structured and production-quality

Evaluates: C++ debugging, security policy interpretation, long-context
           document comprehension, stateful service architecture

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ✅ READY TO USE

  This task is exceptionally well-designed with proper offline setup, pinned
  dependencies, thorough tests, and a challenging multi-faceted debugging
  problem. Ready for deployment without modifications.
================================================================================

Difficulty: ✅ HARD

Status: ✅ Solvable (all tests passed by at least one agent run)

Agent Performance:
  • terminus-claude-opus-4-8: 0.0% (0/5 runs)
  • terminus-gpt5-5: 0.0% (0/5 runs)

Reference Agents:
  • nop: 0.0% (0/1 runs)
  • oracle: 100.0% (3/3 runs)

Failure Breakdown:
  • nop: 1 other
  • terminus-claude-opus-4-8: 1 timeout(s), 4 other
  • terminus-gpt5-5: 5 other

Agent Timeout Gate: ✅ 1/10 real-agent runs timed out (<5; not blocking by itself)

Unit Tests Results:
  • test_health_endpoint_responds: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_matches_resolved_policy_on_random_hosts: 8 passed / 10 runs
  • test_primary_group_membership_grants_sudo: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_locked_and_extended_shells_lose_keys: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_nopasswd_all_and_negation: 8 passed / 10 runs
  • test_sshd_precedence_and_prohibit_password: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_exempt_ceiling_and_roster: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_sudo_defaults_authenticate_grants_passwordless: 8 passed / 10 runs
  • test_sshd_kbdinteractive_alias_first_match: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_sudo_last_match_wins: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_sshd_match_all_resumes_global: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_sudo_includedir_splices_in_order: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_audit_ledger_migrated_and_records_patches: 1 passed / 10 runs
  • test_compliant_host_needs_no_patches: 9 passed / 10 runs

Analysis on Agent Failures:
  • Task Instruction Sufficiency: ❌ FAIL, ## Job Run Summary

### 1. Overall Results

**0 out of 10 trials passed** (reward = 0.0 for all). Every agent scored 13/14 on tests except `tbench-task__Vg9YU4D` (1/14, timed out). The binary scoring system (all-or-nothing) meant that even 93% test accuracy yielded zero reward.

### 2. Common Failure Patterns

**Universal failure — ledger schema migration (10/10 trials):** Every agent failed `test_audit_ledger_migrated_and_records_patches`. The pattern was identical across all trials:
- Agents correctly diagnosed the bug: `ledger.at("entries")` threw because the on-disk file used the legacy key `"audits"` (schema 1)
- Agents "fixed" it by making the code consistent with the on-disk format — writing to `"audits"` and keeping `schema: 1`
- The correct fix was the opposite: migrate the file to schema 2, renaming `"audits"` → `"entries"` and bumping `schema` to `2`

**Secondary failure — `test_nopasswd_all_and_negation`:** Two trials (`tbench-task__9dJ8Gfx`, `tbench-task__aGG2hXD`) also failed sudo negation handling (negated principals not correctly excluded from the passwordless set).

**Tertiary failure — `test_sudo_defaults_authenticate_grants_passwordless`:** One trial (`tbench-task__aGG2hXD`) failed because it added an unnecessary `has_authority` guard requiring both a user spec and a `Defaults` override.

### 3. Hack Check

**No cheating detected — all 10 trials passed `reward_hacking` checks.** No agent accessed `solution/` directories, modified test files, or wrote to reward tracking paths. All failures were legitimate implementation errors.

### 4. Specification Issues (Debug)

There is a genuine **evaluator disagreement** on whether the ledger migration was underspecified:

| Check verdict | Trials |
|---|---|
| **`task_specification: fail`** (spec gap) | `Vg9YU4D`, `JpWLnCy`, `VmB8psG`, `CgBFZs3`, `gobJGVC`, `aGG2hXD`, `kieuNs6` — **7 trials** |
| **`task_specification: pass`** (agent error) | `9dJ8Gfx`, `K2prcwB`, `pJHnuTP` — **3 trials** |

The core debate: instruction.md says *"investigate the file on disk to understand why the current build stopped writing to it"* without explicitly saying schema 2 uses `"entries"`. The "pass" evaluators argue the original `main.cpp` code (`ledger.at("entries")`, `schema = 2`) made the migration inferrable; the "fail" evaluators argue agents could reasonably read the existing on-disk format as ground truth. Given that **10/10 agents made the same wrong inference**, the weight of evidence supports treating this as a **systematic specification gap** — the instruction should explicitly state that schema 2 uses the `"entries"` key and that the on-disk file must be migrated.

### 5. Progress (Failed Trials)

Agents were remarkably close — the policy logic was largely solved:

| Trial | Tests Passed | Key Gap |
|---|---|---|
| `tbench-task__Vg9YU4D` | 1/14 | Timed out; major implementation failures |
| All other 9 trials | **13/14** | Only ledger migration wrong |

The 9 non-timeout trials achieved **92.9% average test pass rate**, failing on a single, specific implementation choice. The task's substantial complexity (statelessness, sudoers alias expansion, sshd precedence, disabled account tokens, etc.) was solved correctly across all these agents.

### 6. Key Observations

- **No model differentiation is possible** — trial names don't indicate distinct models/agents.
- **The timeout outlier** (`tbench-task__Vg9YU4D`) is the only trial that failed broadly; it hit the 1200s limit at step 6, producing a fundamentally incomplete solution. All other agents completed the task.
- **The binary scoring system is punishing** — 93% correctness uniformly yielded 0 reward, masking the fact that these agents solved a genuinely hard task with only one consistent blind spot.

### Recommendation

Fix the task specification: explicitly document that schema 2 stores records under the `"entries"` key and that the migration must rename `"audits"` → `"entries"` on existing on-disk ledgers. This single clarification would likely convert 9/10 trials from 0 reward to full reward.

[Container] 2026/06/26 17:53:30.638637 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found'
Our lab access gateway runs a C++ setup auditor in `/app/cpp-auditor`. It takes a
snapshot of a host's configuration files (passwd, shadow, group, sudoers,
authorized_keys, and the sshd drop-ins) and proposes the patches needed to bring
the host into policy. It exposes `POST /v1/setup/audit` with a request body of
the form `{"files": {...}}` and replies with `{"patches": [...]}`, where each
patch is one of `sudoers.require_password` (target), `ssh.revoke_keys` (target),
or `systemd.set_dropin` (unit/key/value).

The authoritative policy is the **Lab Access Hardening Standard** in
`/app/docs/standard.md`. The auditor has fallen behind the Standard: it predates
several changes and also has a few outright parsing defects, so it now disagrees
with policy on many hosts. The Standard is a body of numbered controls plus an
authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G); where the body and an amendment
conflict, the amendment governs (section 1.4), so several controls don't mean
what their body text alone says. The exact token sets, accepted values, ceilings,
overrides, runas scoping, and ledger schema are defined there as amended.

The auditor's logic lives mainly in `/app/cpp-auditor/src/parse.cpp` (turns the
raw files into a normalized inventory), `/app/cpp-auditor/src/audit.cpp` (turns
that inventory into patches), and `/app/cpp-auditor/src/main.cpp` (the service
and its audit ledger). Make it conform to the Standard so that, for any host
snapshot, `POST /v1/setup/audit` returns exactly the patch set the Standard
implies; a host that is already compliant must produce an empty patch set.
Rebuild with the provided CMake setup (the existing `build/` target) after
editing.

The auditor must also be **stateless**: the patch set returned for a request must
be a pure function of the snapshot in that request, unaffected by any snapshot
audited earlier in the same run. Verification audits many different hosts against
a single long-lived server, so anything that lets one host's data carry over to
another will show up as incorrect patches.

The auditor also keeps a persistent **audit ledger** at
`/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json` and must append every patch it emits, with
earlier records preserved. Operators have noticed the ledger is no longer being
updated even though audits keep returning results. The ledger on disk is in an
older schema than the one the Standard's audit control requires; reconcile it to
the current schema, carrying the existing records forward, so durable logging is
restored.

The behaviors the auditor must get right, all governed by the Standard, are:

- **disabled-account detection** — which `shadow` password tokens, login shells,
  and account-expiry values mark an account disabled;
- **effective group membership** — including members who belong via their
  *primary* group id, not just the textual member list;
- **passwordless-sudo determination** — which `sudoers` rules actually grant it
  (the command scope `NOPASSWD` applies to, the runas target, alias/`%group`/
  negation resolution, last-match ordering, `includedir` splicing, and
  per-user/group `Defaults` overrides);
- **service-account exemptions** — the exempt roster and the user-id ceiling;
- **effective sshd drop-in settings** — the fragment precedence rules and which
  keywords and values are accepted (including the keyboard-interactive keyword
  and its deprecated alias).

The toolchain (cmake, g++) is already installed and everything runs offline.

2026-06-26 17:55:14,724 INFO scripts.test_quality_judge.reviewer: [tbench-task] agent finished (19 turns)
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  Minor

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite thoroughly covers all core constraints through both targeted
fixture-based tests and a randomized property test against a hidden reference
implementation that faithfully encodes the Standard as amended.

Strengths:  The 60-case randomized property test (seeded, deterministic) exercises
every amended clause and compares against a comprehensive reference implementation
that the agent cannot access. Fixture tests pin down each individual behavior.

Weaknesses: The statefulness requirement is only implicitly tested (multiple
requests to one server). The ledger accumulation test only verifies after a
single audit call, not across multiple calls.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite contains 18 test functions covering all core behaviors: disabled-
account detection (locked tokens, nologin shells, expiry), effective group
membership via primary GID, passwordless-sudo with NOPASSWD:ALL scoping,
last-match-wins, includedir splicing, Defaults overrides, host scoping, runas
filtering, sshd Match context evaluation, keyword aliasing, service-account
exemptions, ledger migration, and compliant-host idempotency. The randomized
property test generates diverse host configurations exercising every amendment
and compares against a hidden reference implementation the agent cannot access.
No shortcut exists: the agent must actually fix the C++ auditor's parsing and
audit logic to match the resolved policy across arbitrary inputs.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Statefulness tested only implicitly via random-host ordering
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:27-34
Problem: The instruction explicitly requires that the auditor be stateless —
"the patch set returned for a request must be a pure function of the snapshot in
that request, unaffected by any snapshot audited earlier in the same run." While
the 60 randomized requests hitting a single server would likely catch a
statefulness bug (since earlier host data would corrupt later results), there is
no dedicated test that sends two known snapshots in sequence and asserts the
second result is unaffected by the first.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  for _ in range(RANDOM_CASES):                                             │
│      snap = make_snapshot(rng)                                             │
│      if canon(cpp_audit(snap)) != canon(expected_patches(snap)):           │
│          mismatches += 1                                                   │
│  assert mismatches == 0                                                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def test_statelessness_across_audits():                                   │
│      snap_a = load_fixture("sudo_nopasswd.json")                           │
│      snap_b = load_fixture("compliant.json")                               │
│      cpp_audit(snap_a)  # prime with non-empty result                      │
│      assert cpp_audit(snap_b) == []  # must not leak from snap_a           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: A subtle statefulness bug that only manifests on specific input
sequences could survive the random test if none of the 60 generated hosts
happen to trigger the leak pattern. A dedicated two-request test with known
expected outputs would be more targeted, though in practice the random test
provides strong coverage.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Ledger accumulation not tested across multiple audit calls
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:212-234
Problem: The instruction requires the auditor to "append every patch it emits"
across all requests. The test only verifies the ledger after a single audit
call. An implementation that overwrites the ledger on each request (keeping
only the latest patches plus legacy) would pass the test, since earlier tests
do not check the ledger.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  snap = load_fixture("sudo_nopasswd.json")                                 │
│  patches = cpp_audit(snap)                                                 │
│  # ... checks ledger contains legacy + patches from this one call          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  snap1 = load_fixture("primary_gid.json")                                  │
│  patches1 = cpp_audit(snap1)                                               │
│  snap2 = load_fixture("sudo_nopasswd.json")                                │
│  patches2 = cpp_audit(snap2)                                               │
│  with open(LEDGER_PATH) as fh:                                             │
│      ledger = json.load(fh)                                                │
│  recorded = {json.dumps(e, sort_keys=True) for e in ledger["entries"]}     │
│  for p in patches1 + patches2:                                             │
│      assert json.dumps(p, sort_keys=True) in recorded                      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: An auditor that replaces rather than accumulates ledger entries
would still pass the current test. However, since the random property test
runs 60 audits before the ledger test, a replace-only implementation may
still fail if test ordering guarantees the ledger test runs last (pytest
runs in file order by default). The gap is minor but worth noting.

================================================================================

Our lab access gateway runs a C++ setup auditor in `/app/cpp-auditor`. It takes a
snapshot of a host's configuration files (passwd, shadow, group, sudoers,
authorized_keys, and the sshd drop-ins) and proposes the patches needed to bring
the host into policy. It exposes `POST /v1/setup/audit` with a request body of
the form `{"files": {...}}` and replies with `{"patches": [...]}`, where each
patch is one of `sudoers.require_password` (target), `ssh.revoke_keys` (target),
or `systemd.set_dropin` (unit/key/value).

The authoritative policy is the **Lab Access Hardening Standard** in
`/app/docs/standard.md`. The auditor has fallen behind the Standard: it predates
several changes and also has a few outright parsing defects, so it now disagrees
with policy on many hosts. The Standard is a body of numbered controls plus an
authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G); where the body and an amendment
conflict, the amendment governs (section 1.4), so several controls don't mean
what their body text alone says. The exact token sets, accepted values, ceilings,
overrides, runas scoping, and ledger schema are defined there as amended.

The auditor's logic lives mainly in `/app/cpp-auditor/src/parse.cpp` (turns the
raw files into a normalized inventory), `/app/cpp-auditor/src/audit.cpp` (turns
that inventory into patches), and `/app/cpp-auditor/src/main.cpp` (the service
and its audit ledger). Make it conform to the Standard so that, for any host
snapshot, `POST /v1/setup/audit` returns exactly the patch set the Standard
implies; a host that is already compliant must produce an empty patch set.
Rebuild with the provided CMake setup (the existing `build/` target) after
editing.

The auditor must also be **stateless**: the patch set returned for a request must
be a pure function of the snapshot in that request, unaffected by any snapshot
audited earlier in the same run. Verification audits many different hosts against
a single long-lived server, so anything that lets one host's data carry over to
another will show up as incorrect patches.

The auditor also keeps a persistent **audit ledger** at
`/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json` and must append every patch it emits, with
earlier records preserved. Operators have noticed the ledger is no longer being
updated even though audits keep returning results. The ledger on disk is in an
older schema than the one the Standard's audit control requires; reconcile it to
the current schema, carrying the existing records forward, so durable logging is
restored.

The behaviors the auditor must get right, all governed by the Standard, are:

- **disabled-account detection** — which `shadow` password tokens, login shells,
  and account-expiry values mark an account disabled;
- **effective group membership** — including members who belong via their
  *primary* group id, not just the textual member list;
- **passwordless-sudo determination** — which `sudoers` rules actually grant it
  (the command scope `NOPASSWD` applies to, the runas target, alias/`%group`/
  negation resolution, last-match ordering, `includedir` splicing, and
  per-user/group `Defaults` overrides);
- **service-account exemptions** — the exempt roster and the user-id ceiling;
- **effective sshd drop-in settings** — the fragment precedence rules and which
  keywords and values are accepted (including the keyboard-interactive keyword
  and its deprecated alias).

The toolchain (cmake, g++) is already installed and everything runs offline.

This task requires an agent to repair a C++ security auditor that parses host
configuration snapshots (passwd, shadow, sudoers, sshd) and produces
remediation patches per a detailed hardening standard. The environment ships a
deliberately broken/outdated version of the auditor; the solution replaces the
three core source files (parse.cpp, audit.cpp, main.cpp) with corrected
implementations and rebuilds. The test suite contains 17 targeted fixture-based
tests plus a 60-iteration randomized property test that validates the auditor
against a reference implementation hidden in the test helpers.

Current approach: Directory is named "tbench-task"

Suggested fix: Rename to "repair-cpp-setup-auditor" or similar descriptive name
that conveys the task's domain and action.

identification in the benchmark suite. The task.toml and job logs already
reference "repair-cpp-setup-auditor" as the internal name.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

one-line docstring but doesn't explain what behaviors the randomized snapshots
exercise or how failures should be debugged.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

scope of the fuzz test and debug regressions.

This is a high-quality, challenging security-domain debugging task that
exercises deep C++ comprehension, policy interpretation across a dense
standards document, and correct parsing of multiple Unix configuration formats.

broken-code starting point, and comprehensive verification.

================================================================================

✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solve.sh copies pre-written corrected source files (parse.cpp, audit.cpp, main.cpp) into /app/cpp-auditor/src/ and then rebuilds the project with cmake. ✅ pass - structured_data_schema: instruction.md explicitly documents the API schema: POST /v1/setup/audit, request body {files: {...}}, response {patches: [...]}, and all three patch types with their fields (sudoers.require_password with target, ssh.revoke_keys with target, systemd.set_dropin with unit/key/value). The ledger schema (schema 2, entries array) is documented in the Standard (AU-LEDGER control in standard.md), which instruction.md designates as the authoritative contract, and the Standard is available to the agent at /app/docs/standard.md.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: The base Docker image is pinned by SHA256 digest. All apt packages are pinned with exact version strings (e.g. g++=4:12.2.0-3, cmake=3.25.1-1, nlohmann-json3-dev=3.11.2-2). Python packages in requirements.txt are pinned with exact versions and SHA256 hashes, installed with --require-hashes --no-deps. The vendored cpp-httplib header (v0.15.3) is checked in directly with no network fetch needed.
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names. The AUDITOR_PORT environment variable is consistently named. File paths in helpers.py (/app/fixtures/), the Dockerfile COPYs, and test.sh references all align. The AUDITOR_LEDGER env var name used in test_outputs.py matches what main.cpp would read. The CMake target name (setup_auditor) matches what test.sh invokes.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only copies cpp-auditor/, fixtures/, and docs/ into the image. The tests/ and solution/ directories are never referenced in the Dockerfile and are not copied into the runtime environment.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solve.sh copies pre-written corrected source files (parse.cpp, audit.cpp, main.cpp) into /app/cpp-auditor/src/ and then rebuilds the project with cmake. The resulting binary genuinely processes incoming HTTP requests, parses snapshots, applies the resolved policy logic, and returns computed patches — it does not simply print a hardcoded answer. The solution demonstrates the engineering steps an agent would actually perform: identify which source files need changes, apply fixes, and rebuild.
➖ not_applicable - file_reference_mentioned: This task does not require the agent to produce output files for tests to check. The agent produces a rebuilt C++ binary that serves HTTP requests; tests interact with it via the live API. The only file checked by tests is the audit ledger (/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json), which instruction.md explicitly mentions by path as the persistent audit ledger location.

Our lab access gateway runs a C++ setup auditor in `/app/cpp-auditor`. It takes a
snapshot of a host's configuration files (passwd, shadow, group, sudoers,
authorized_keys, and the sshd drop-ins) and proposes the patches needed to bring
the host into policy. It exposes `POST /v1/setup/audit` with a request body of
the form `{"files": {...}}` and replies with `{"patches": [...]}`, where each
patch is one of `sudoers.require_password` (target), `ssh.revoke_keys` (target),
or `systemd.set_dropin` (unit/key/value).

The authoritative policy is the **Lab Access Hardening Standard** in
`/app/docs/standard.md`. The auditor has fallen behind the Standard: it predates
several changes and also has a few outright parsing defects, so it now disagrees
with policy on many hosts. The Standard is a body of numbered controls plus an
authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G); where the body and an amendment
conflict, the amendment governs (section 1.4), so several controls don't mean
what their body text alone says. The exact token sets, accepted values, ceilings,
overrides, runas scoping, and ledger schema are defined there as amended.

The auditor's logic lives mainly in `/app/cpp-auditor/src/parse.cpp` (turns the
raw files into a normalized inventory), `/app/cpp-auditor/src/audit.cpp` (turns
that inventory into patches), and `/app/cpp-auditor/src/main.cpp` (the service
and its audit ledger). Make it conform to the Standard so that, for any host
snapshot, `POST /v1/setup/audit` returns exactly the patch set the Standard
implies; a host that is already compliant must produce an empty patch set.
Rebuild with the provided CMake setup (the existing `build/` target) after
editing.

The auditor must also be **stateless**: the patch set returned for a request must
be a pure function of the snapshot in that request, unaffected by any snapshot
audited earlier in the same run. Verification audits many different hosts against
a single long-lived server, so anything that lets one host's data carry over to
another will show up as incorrect patches.

The auditor also keeps a persistent **audit ledger** at
`/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json` and must append every patch it emits, with
earlier records preserved. Operators have noticed the ledger is no longer being
updated even though audits keep returning results. The ledger on disk is in an
older schema than the one the Standard's audit control requires; reconcile it to
the current schema, carrying the existing records forward, so durable logging is
restored.

The behaviors the auditor must get right, all governed by the Standard, are:

- **disabled-account detection** — which `shadow` password tokens, login shells,
  and account-expiry values mark an account disabled;
- **effective group membership** — including members who belong via their
  *primary* group id, not just the textual member list;
- **passwordless-sudo determination** — which `sudoers` rules actually grant it
  (the command scope `NOPASSWD` applies to, the runas target, alias/`%group`/
  negation resolution, last-match ordering, `includedir` splicing, and
  per-user/group `Defaults` overrides);
- **service-account exemptions** — the exempt roster and the user-id ceiling;
- **effective sshd drop-in settings** — the fragment precedence rules and which
  keywords and values are accepted (including the keyboard-interactive keyword
  and its deprecated alias).

The toolchain (cmake, g++) is already installed and everything runs offline.

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  Minor

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite thoroughly covers all core constraints through both targeted
fixture-based tests and a randomized property test against a hidden reference
implementation that faithfully encodes the Standard as amended.

Strengths:  The 60-case randomized property test (seeded, deterministic) exercises
every amended clause and compares against a comprehensive reference implementation
that the agent cannot access. Fixture tests pin down each individual behavior.

Weaknesses: The statefulness requirement is only implicitly tested (multiple
requests to one server). The ledger accumulation test only verifies after a
single audit call, not across multiple calls.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite contains 18 test functions covering all core behaviors: disabled-
account detection (locked tokens, nologin shells, expiry), effective group
membership via primary GID, passwordless-sudo with NOPASSWD:ALL scoping,
last-match-wins, includedir splicing, Defaults overrides, host scoping, runas
filtering, sshd Match context evaluation, keyword aliasing, service-account
exemptions, ledger migration, and compliant-host idempotency. The randomized
property test generates diverse host configurations exercising every amendment
and compares against a hidden reference implementation the agent cannot access.
No shortcut exists: the agent must actually fix the C++ auditor's parsing and
audit logic to match the resolved policy across arbitrary inputs.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Statefulness tested only implicitly via random-host ordering
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:27-34
Problem: The instruction explicitly requires that the auditor be stateless —
"the patch set returned for a request must be a pure function of the snapshot in
that request, unaffected by any snapshot audited earlier in the same run." While
the 60 randomized requests hitting a single server would likely catch a
statefulness bug (since earlier host data would corrupt later results), there is
no dedicated test that sends two known snapshots in sequence and asserts the
second result is unaffected by the first.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  for _ in range(RANDOM_CASES):                                             │
│      snap = make_snapshot(rng)                                             │
│      if canon(cpp_audit(snap)) != canon(expected_patches(snap)):           │
│          mismatches += 1                                                   │
│  assert mismatches == 0                                                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def test_statelessness_across_audits():                                   │
│      snap_a = load_fixture("sudo_nopasswd.json")                           │
│      snap_b = load_fixture("compliant.json")                               │
│      cpp_audit(snap_a)  # prime with non-empty result                      │
│      assert cpp_audit(snap_b) == []  # must not leak from snap_a           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: A subtle statefulness bug that only manifests on specific input
sequences could survive the random test if none of the 60 generated hosts
happen to trigger the leak pattern. A dedicated two-request test with known
expected outputs would be more targeted, though in practice the random test
provides strong coverage.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Ledger accumulation not tested across multiple audit calls
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:212-234
Problem: The instruction requires the auditor to "append every patch it emits"
across all requests. The test only verifies the ledger after a single audit
call. An implementation that overwrites the ledger on each request (keeping
only the latest patches plus legacy) would pass the test, since earlier tests
do not check the ledger.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  snap = load_fixture("sudo_nopasswd.json")                                 │
│  patches = cpp_audit(snap)                                                 │
│  # ... checks ledger contains legacy + patches from this one call          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  snap1 = load_fixture("primary_gid.json")                                  │
│  patches1 = cpp_audit(snap1)                                               │
│  snap2 = load_fixture("sudo_nopasswd.json")                                │
│  patches2 = cpp_audit(snap2)                                               │
│  with open(LEDGER_PATH) as fh:                                             │
│      ledger = json.load(fh)                                                │
│  recorded = {json.dumps(e, sort_keys=True) for e in ledger["entries"]}     │
│  for p in patches1 + patches2:                                             │
│      assert json.dumps(p, sort_keys=True) in recorded                      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: An auditor that replaces rather than accumulates ledger entries
would still pass the current test. However, since the random property test
runs 60 audits before the ledger test, a replace-only implementation may
still fail if test ordering guarantees the ledger test runs last (pytest
runs in file order by default). The gap is minor but worth noting.

================================================================================
