## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: instruction.md clearly specifies the API contract (exact request/response JSON shapes, patch object keys), the files to edit, the rebuild command, the statelessness requirement, the ledger migration (schema 1→2, 'audits'→'entries' key), and all policy behaviors the agent must implement — including the exact audit context (user root, IP 198.51.100.10, host gw-lab-01) for sshd Match evaluation and sudoers host scoping. For policy details that are too voluminous to enumerate (exact token sets, uid ceilings, exempt roster), the instruction correctly redirects the agent to the Standard in /app/docs/standard.md. Every behavior tested in test_outputs.py corresponds to something explicitly stated or clearly implied by instruction.md.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: The test suite covers all behaviors described in instruction.md: health endpoint, random policy compliance (60 cases), primary GID membership, disabled-account detection (*LK*, git-shell, bare '!'), NOPASSWD+negation, sshd drop-in precedence with Match context, exempt ceiling/roster, Defaults !authenticate, KbdInteractive alias, last-match-wins, Match all, combined Match criteria, includedir splicing, runas non-root, account expiry, host scoping with aliases, ledger migration (schema 1→2, legacy record preserved, patches appended), and compliant-host no-op. The 60-case random test exercises nearly all code paths in combination.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: test_outputs.py uses individual pytest functions each with a clear docstring explaining what is being tested and why (e.g., 'ChallengeResponseAuthentication aliases KbdInteractiveAuthentication, first wins'). helpers.py is well-organized with section comments (HTTP client, reference implementation, randomized snapshot generator) and inline amendment references (G-2026-xx) tying each constant and rule back to the policy document. The structure is readable and maintainable.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: helpers.py (the reference implementation) lives in tests/ which is NOT copied into the Docker image — the Dockerfile only copies cpp-auditor/, fixtures/, and docs/. The solution/ directory is also absent from the image. The oracle.txt files shown to the agent only contain cmake build output. The test.sh resets the ledger to the legacy schema-1 state before tests run. The agent must independently read and interpret the long standard.md document (with filler controls obscuring the normative ones) to derive the correct policy, making trivial string-matching shortcuts infeasible.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: instruction.md explicitly and normatively documents: the request shape ({"files": {...}} with each sub-field named), the response shape ({"patches": [...]}), all three patch object types with exact key names and value placeholders, and both ledger schemas ({"schema": 1, "audits": [...]} and {"schema": 2, "entries": [...]}). These are stated as requirements ('Keep this request and patch shape exactly; don't rename the keys'), not merely as illustrative examples.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: Python pip dependencies are fully pinned with SHA-256 hashes in requirements.txt using --require-hashes. The base Docker image is digest-pinned (sha256:01f42367...), which effectively pins the apt package snapshot. The cpp-httplib header is vendored at v0.15.3 with no network fetch. nlohmann-json3-dev is an apt package left unpinned, which is acceptable given the digest-pinned base.
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names. All paths referenced in instruction.md (/app/cpp-auditor/src/*.cpp, /app/docs/standard.md, /app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json) match what the Dockerfile places at those locations. JSON keys in patch objects and fixture files are consistent throughout. The test.sh loop uses `_` as the loop variable, which is unconventional but syntactically valid.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only COPYs cpp-auditor/, fixtures/, and docs/ into the image. Neither tests/ nor solution/ is included in the COPY directives. The test runner mounts tests from /tests inside the container at verification time (separate from the build image), so the agent has no access to the reference implementation in helpers.py or the expected outputs.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solution/solve.sh copies three corrected C++ source files (parse.cpp, audit.cpp, main.cpp) to /app/cpp-auditor/src/ and rebuilds the binary with cmake. The solution files implement full parsing and policy logic — they derive patches by parsing passwd/shadow/group/sudoers/sshd_config data and applying rules. The solve.sh does not echo or cat any pre-computed answers; it installs corrected source code and triggers compilation.
✅ pass - file_reference_mentioned: The only file the tests check on disk is the audit ledger at /app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json, which is explicitly named in instruction.md: 'The auditor also keeps a persistent audit ledger at /app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json'. All other verification is done via HTTP to the running service, not by reading output files.

  • Task Instruction Sufficiency: ✅ PASS, ## Job Summary

### 1. Overall Results
- **2 trials run, 0 passed** (both received reward 0.0)
- Both trials tackled the same task: repairing a C++ policy auditor against a complex standard with Appendix G amendments
- No model/agent differentiation is noted between trials — both appear to use the same agent type

### 2. Common Failure Patterns
Both trials failed on the **same two test categories**, suggesting systematic implementation gaps:

| Test | tbench-task__m3NhJ6q | tbench-task__k24z6S6 |
|---|---|---|
| `test_nopasswd_all_and_negation` | ❌ Incorrect negation principal handling | ✅ Passed |
| `test_matches_resolved_policy_on_random_hosts` | ❌ 6/60 mismatches | ❌ 13/60 mismatches |

The **randomized host conformance test** was the universal blocker. Both agents handled all named fixture tests well but broke down on edge cases exposed by random host generation — indicating their implementations were overfit to the known test cases rather than fully generalizing the policy logic.

### 3. Hack Check
**Clean — no cheating detected.** Both trials passed `reward_hacking` checks. Agents worked legitimately: reading source files and the policy standard, writing replacement C++ via Python scripting, rebuilding with cmake, and smoke-testing via HTTP. Neither agent attempted to access solution directories, manipulate test files, or write to reward files.

### 4. Instruction/Specification Issues
**None detected.** Both trials passed `task_specification` checks. The reviewers confirmed that `instruction.md` and `standard.md` contained all necessary information, including Appendix G amendments covering negation resolution and the NOPASSWD:ALL narrowing. Failures were attributed to **implementation bugs**, not spec gaps — the named fixture tests passing in both trials confirms the specification was legible and sufficient.

### 5. Progress on Failed Trials
Agents got surprisingly far:
- **tbench-task__m3NhJ6q**: 16/18 tests passing (~89%)
- **tbench-task__k24z6S6**: 17/18 named tests + ledger migration (~94%), but 13/60 random mismatches

The all-or-nothing reward function (0.0 for anything short of full pass) masks what was genuinely strong performance. Both agents successfully implemented: ledger schema migration, disabled-account detection, effective group membership, sshd drop-in settings, service-account exemptions, and most Appendix G amendments. The gap was narrowly in **policy resolution across host-scoped edge cases** in the random conformance test.

### 6. Key Differences Between Trials
- **k24z6S6 was marginally stronger** on named tests (17/18 vs 16/18 — it also passed `test_nopasswd_all_and_negation` which m3NhJ6q failed)
- **k24z6S6 was weaker** on random conformance (13/60 mismatches vs 6/60), suggesting its negation fix may have introduced a regression in broader host-scoping logic
- Both trials used a similar approach (Python-scripted string-replace patches + cmake rebuild), suggesting the agent strategy was consistent but the implementation details diverged in the final polish

### Recommendation
The core unresolved issue is **host-scoped policy resolution under random inputs**. A targeted fix would focus on: (a) last-match-wins semantics across host-scoped `sudoers` entries, and (b) correct handling of negated principals in `NOPASSWD` grants. These are the two behaviors that separated near-complete from complete implementations.

[Container] 2026/06/26 20:16:03.051564 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found; task may use steps/*/instruction.md'
Our lab access gateway runs a C++ setup auditor in `/app/cpp-auditor`. It takes a
snapshot of a host's configuration files and proposes the patches needed to bring
the host into policy. It exposes `POST /v1/setup/audit` with a request body
`{"files": {...}}`, where `files` carries `passwd`, `shadow`, `group`, and
`sudoers` as raw file-content strings, `authorized_keys` as an object mapping
each username to that user's key-file text, and `sshd_config.d` as an object
mapping each drop-in filename to its text. It replies with `{"patches": [...]}`,
where every patch is an object keyed by `action`: either
`{"action": "sudoers.require_password", "target": "<user>"}`,
`{"action": "ssh.revoke_keys", "target": "<user>"}`, or
`{"action": "systemd.set_dropin", "unit": "sshd", "key": "<keyword>", "value": "<value>"}`.
Keep this request and patch shape exactly; don't rename the keys.

The authoritative policy is the **Lab Access Hardening Standard** in
`/app/docs/standard.md`. The auditor has fallen behind the Standard: it predates
several changes and also has a few outright parsing defects, so it now disagrees
with policy on many hosts. The Standard is a body of numbered controls plus an
authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G); where the body and an amendment
conflict, the amendment governs (section 1.4), so several controls don't mean
what their body text alone says. The exact token sets, accepted values, ceilings,
overrides, runas scoping, and ledger schema are defined there as amended.

The auditor's logic lives mainly in `/app/cpp-auditor/src/parse.cpp` (turns the
raw files into a normalized inventory), `/app/cpp-auditor/src/audit.cpp` (turns
that inventory into patches), and `/app/cpp-auditor/src/main.cpp` (the service
and its audit ledger). Make it conform to the Standard so that, for any host
snapshot, `POST /v1/setup/audit` returns exactly the patch set the Standard
implies; a host that is already compliant must produce an empty patch set.
Rebuild with the provided CMake setup (the existing `build/` target) after
editing.

The auditor must also be **stateless**: the patch set returned for a request must
be a pure function of the snapshot in that request, unaffected by any snapshot
audited earlier in the same run. Verification audits many different hosts against
a single long-lived server, so anything that lets one host's data carry over to
another will show up as incorrect patches.

The auditor also keeps a persistent **audit ledger** at
`/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json` and must append every patch it emits, with
earlier records preserved. Operators have noticed the ledger is no longer being
updated even though audits keep returning results. The ledger on disk is in the
older `{"schema": 1, "audits": [...]}` layout; the Standard's audit control now
requires `{"schema": 2, "entries": [...]}`. Reconcile it to that current schema,
carrying every existing record forward into `entries` (appended patches use the
same patch objects described above), so durable logging is restored.

The behaviors the auditor must get right, all governed by the Standard, are:

- **disabled-account detection** — which `shadow` password tokens, login shells,
  and account-expiry values mark an account disabled;
- **effective group membership** — including members who belong via their
  *primary* group id, not just the textual member list;
- **passwordless-sudo determination** — which `sudoers` rules actually grant it
  (the command scope `NOPASSWD` applies to, the runas target, alias/`%group`/
  negation resolution, last-match ordering, `includedir` splicing, and
  per-user/group `Defaults` overrides);
- **service-account exemptions** — the exempt roster and the user-id ceiling;
- **effective sshd drop-in settings** — the fragment precedence rules and which
  keywords and values are accepted (including the keyboard-interactive keyword
  and its deprecated alias); evaluate `Match` blocks for the fixed audit context
  of connecting user `root` from source address `198.51.100.10` on host
  `gw-lab-01`, which is also the host the `sudoers` host scoping resolves against.

The toolchain (cmake, g++) is already installed and everything runs offline.

SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires agents to fix a buggy C++ security auditor that parses host
configuration snapshots (passwd, shadow, sudoers, sshd drop-ins) and proposes
remediation patches per a detailed hardening standard with amendments. The
solution patches three C++ source files to handle missing features (extended
lock tokens, account expiry, sudoers negation/last-match, host scoping, sshd
Match blocks) and architectural defects (shared state across requests, broken
ledger migration). The test suite uses 17 targeted fixture-based tests plus 60
randomized host snapshots verified against a hidden reference implementation.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Filename Overlap Between Solution and Environment (Sub-check A)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/solution/ and tbench-task/environment/cpp-auditor/src/
Problem: The filenames parse.cpp, audit.cpp, and main.cpp appear in both the
         solution/ directory and the environment/cpp-auditor/src/ directory.
         This is expected for a fix/debug task where the environment ships the
         broken version, but reviewers should confirm the environment files are
         NOT the corrected versions.

Current approach: The environment contains intentionally broken C++ source
files (missing shells, wrong UID ceiling, no Match-block support, stale
caching, broken ledger). The solution ships corrected replacements.

Explanation: Diff analysis confirms the environment files are meaningfully
broken — they lack git-shell/sync shells, *LK* token, account expiry,
sshd KbdInteractiveAuthentication, correct exempt roster, host scoping,
and the statelessness fix. This is NOT leakage; it is the intended task
design for a debugging/fix task. No action required.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. test.sh Uses `set -uo pipefail` Instead of `set -euo pipefail`
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test.sh (line 2)
Problem: The script uses `set -uo pipefail` (missing `-e`). While this is
         intentional here (the script handles exit codes manually and must
         always write reward.txt), it could mask unexpected failures in the
         cmake build steps before the pytest invocation.

Current approach: The script captures cmake build failure via `$?` check and
writes reward 0 on failure, then runs pytest and writes reward based on its
exit code.

Suggested fix: No change strictly required — the manual error handling is
correct and reward.txt is always written. However, consider adding explicit
error checks after the cmake configure step (line 11) as well:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  cmake -S /app/cpp-auditor -B /app/cpp-auditor/build \                      │
│      >/tmp/cmake_configure.log 2>&1                                         │
│  if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then                                                      │
│      echo "CMake configure failed" >&2                                      │
│      echo 0 > /logs/verifier/reward.txt                                     │
│      exit 0                                                                 │
│  fi                                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Currently only the build step (line 12-18) is guarded. If cmake
configure fails, the build step would also fail and catch it, but an explicit
check would give clearer diagnostics.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Consider Adding Test Docstrings to Randomized Test
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test_outputs.py (line 27)

Current approach: `test_matches_resolved_policy_on_random_hosts` has a
one-line docstring but produces opaque failure messages ("X/60 snapshots
disagreed with policy") without identifying which specific policy clauses
failed.

Suggested improvement:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def test_matches_resolved_policy_on_random_hosts():                        │
│      """Across randomized host snapshots the auditor agrees with the        │
│      resolved policy (all amended clauses exercised)."""                     │
│      rng = random.Random(20260625)                                          │
│      failures = []                                                          │
│      for i in range(RANDOM_CASES):                                          │
│          snap = make_snapshot(rng)                                           │
│          got = canon(cpp_audit(snap))                                        │
│          want = canon(expected_patches(snap))                                │
│          if got != want:                                                     │
│              failures.append(f"case {i}: got {got}, want {want}")            │
│      assert not failures, "\n".join(failures[:5])                            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: When the randomized test fails, including the first few mismatched
cases in the assertion message would help debugging without exposing the full
reference implementation to the agent (since tests aren't visible at runtime).

================================================================================

This is an excellent, deeply technical Terminal-Bench task that tests an
agent's ability to comprehend a complex security standard with amendments,
diagnose multiple interacting C++ bugs, and implement compliant fixes across

    guard (caught transitively by the build step)

Evaluates: C++ debugging, security policy interpretation, systems programming,

[Container] 2026/06/26 20:16:03.051564 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found; task may use steps/*/instruction.md'
Our lab access gateway runs a C++ setup auditor in `/app/cpp-auditor`. It takes a
snapshot of a host's configuration files and proposes the patches needed to bring
the host into policy. It exposes `POST /v1/setup/audit` with a request body
`{"files": {...}}`, where `files` carries `passwd`, `shadow`, `group`, and
`sudoers` as raw file-content strings, `authorized_keys` as an object mapping
each username to that user's key-file text, and `sshd_config.d` as an object
mapping each drop-in filename to its text. It replies with `{"patches": [...]}`,
where every patch is an object keyed by `action`: either
`{"action": "sudoers.require_password", "target": "<user>"}`,
`{"action": "ssh.revoke_keys", "target": "<user>"}`, or
`{"action": "systemd.set_dropin", "unit": "sshd", "key": "<keyword>", "value": "<value>"}`.
Keep this request and patch shape exactly; don't rename the keys.

The authoritative policy is the **Lab Access Hardening Standard** in
`/app/docs/standard.md`. The auditor has fallen behind the Standard: it predates
several changes and also has a few outright parsing defects, so it now disagrees
with policy on many hosts. The Standard is a body of numbered controls plus an
authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G); where the body and an amendment
conflict, the amendment governs (section 1.4), so several controls don't mean
what their body text alone says. The exact token sets, accepted values, ceilings,
overrides, runas scoping, and ledger schema are defined there as amended.

The auditor's logic lives mainly in `/app/cpp-auditor/src/parse.cpp` (turns the
raw files into a normalized inventory), `/app/cpp-auditor/src/audit.cpp` (turns
that inventory into patches), and `/app/cpp-auditor/src/main.cpp` (the service
and its audit ledger). Make it conform to the Standard so that, for any host
snapshot, `POST /v1/setup/audit` returns exactly the patch set the Standard
implies; a host that is already compliant must produce an empty patch set.
Rebuild with the provided CMake setup (the existing `build/` target) after
editing.

The auditor must also be **stateless**: the patch set returned for a request must
be a pure function of the snapshot in that request, unaffected by any snapshot
audited earlier in the same run. Verification audits many different hosts against
a single long-lived server, so anything that lets one host's data carry over to
another will show up as incorrect patches.

The auditor also keeps a persistent **audit ledger** at
`/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json` and must append every patch it emits, with
earlier records preserved. Operators have noticed the ledger is no longer being
updated even though audits keep returning results. The ledger on disk is in the
older `{"schema": 1, "audits": [...]}` layout; the Standard's audit control now
requires `{"schema": 2, "entries": [...]}`. Reconcile it to that current schema,
carrying every existing record forward into `entries` (appended patches use the
same patch objects described above), so durable logging is restored.

The behaviors the auditor must get right, all governed by the Standard, are:

- **disabled-account detection** — which `shadow` password tokens, login shells,
  and account-expiry values mark an account disabled;
- **effective group membership** — including members who belong via their
  *primary* group id, not just the textual member list;
- **passwordless-sudo determination** — which `sudoers` rules actually grant it
  (the command scope `NOPASSWD` applies to, the runas target, alias/`%group`/
  negation resolution, last-match ordering, `includedir` splicing, and
  per-user/group `Defaults` overrides);
- **service-account exemptions** — the exempt roster and the user-id ceiling;
- **effective sshd drop-in settings** — the fragment precedence rules and which
  keywords and values are accepted (including the keyboard-interactive keyword
  and its deprecated alias); evaluate `Match` blocks for the fixed audit context
  of connecting user `root` from source address `198.51.100.10` on host
  `gw-lab-01`, which is also the host the `sudoers` host scoping resolves against.

The toolchain (cmake, g++) is already installed and everything runs offline.

python3 -c "import json; rubric=json.load(open('output/rubric.json')); review=json.load(open('output/review.json')); json.dump({**rubric, **review}, open('output/message.json', 'w'))"

cat output/message.json
aws s3 cp output/message.json ${OUTPUT_S3_URI}

# Rubric 1
Agent identifies and fixes the non-interactive shell set by adding /usr/bin/git-shell and /bin/sync per amendment G-2026-01, +2
Agent adds *LK* as a recognized locked-password token per amendment G-2026-01, +1
Agent implements account-expiry detection using the shadow 8th field and reference day 20620 per amendment G-2026-16, +2
Agent fixes effective_group_members to include accounts whose primary GID matches the group's GID (AC-GROUP-EFFECTIVE), +3
Agent narrows NOPASSWD detection to require NOPASSWD: ALL (not just any occurrence of NOPASSWD) per amendment G-2026-02, +3
Agent implements last-match-wins semantics for sudoers principal passwordless state per amendment G-2026-07, +3
Agent implements Defaults:<binder> !authenticate as a passwordless-sudo grant per amendment G-2026-05, +2
Agent implements #includedir / @includedir splicing of sudoers.d files in sorted order per amendment G-2026-09, +2

SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires agents to fix a buggy C++ security auditor that parses host
configuration snapshots (passwd, shadow, sudoers, sshd drop-ins) and proposes
remediation patches per a detailed hardening standard with amendments. The
solution patches three C++ source files to handle missing features (extended
lock tokens, account expiry, sudoers negation/last-match, host scoping, sshd
Match blocks) and architectural defects (shared state across requests, broken
ledger migration). The test suite uses 17 targeted fixture-based tests plus 60
randomized host snapshots verified against a hidden reference implementation.

1. Filename Overlap Between Solution and Environment (Sub-check A)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/solution/ and tbench-task/environment/cpp-auditor/src/
Problem: The filenames parse.cpp, audit.cpp, and main.cpp appear in both the
         solution/ directory and the environment/cpp-auditor/src/ directory.
         This is expected for a fix/debug task where the environment ships the
         broken version, but reviewers should confirm the environment files are
         NOT the corrected versions.

Current approach: The environment contains intentionally broken C++ source
files (missing shells, wrong UID ceiling, no Match-block support, stale
caching, broken ledger). The solution ships corrected replacements.

Explanation: Diff analysis confirms the environment files are meaningfully
broken — they lack git-shell/sync shells, *LK* token, account expiry,
sshd KbdInteractiveAuthentication, correct exempt roster, host scoping,
and the statelessness fix. This is NOT leakage; it is the intended task
design for a debugging/fix task. No action required.

2. test.sh Uses `set -uo pipefail` Instead of `set -euo pipefail`
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test.sh (line 2)
Problem: The script uses `set -uo pipefail` (missing `-e`). While this is
         intentional here (the script handles exit codes manually and must
         always write reward.txt), it could mask unexpected failures in the
         cmake build steps before the pytest invocation.

Current approach: The script captures cmake build failure via `$?` check and
writes reward 0 on failure, then runs pytest and writes reward based on its
exit code.

Suggested fix: No change strictly required — the manual error handling is
correct and reward.txt is always written. However, consider adding explicit
error checks after the cmake configure step (line 11) as well:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  cmake -S /app/cpp-auditor -B /app/cpp-auditor/build \                      │
│      >/tmp/cmake_configure.log 2>&1                                         │
│  if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then                                                      │
│      echo "CMake configure failed" >&2                                      │
│      echo 0 > /logs/verifier/reward.txt                                     │
│      exit 0                                                                 │
│  fi                                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Currently only the build step (line 12-18) is guarded. If cmake
configure fails, the build step would also fail and catch it, but an explicit
check would give clearer diagnostics.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Consider Adding Test Docstrings to Randomized Test
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test_outputs.py (line 27)

Current approach: `test_matches_resolved_policy_on_random_hosts` has a
one-line docstring but produces opaque failure messages ("X/60 snapshots
disagreed with policy") without identifying which specific policy clauses
failed.

Suggested improvement:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def test_matches_resolved_policy_on_random_hosts():                        │
│      """Across randomized host snapshots the auditor agrees with the        │
│      resolved policy (all amended clauses exercised)."""                     │
│      rng = random.Random(20260625)                                          │
│      failures = []                                                          │
│      for i in range(RANDOM_CASES):                                          │
│          snap = make_snapshot(rng)                                           │
│          got = canon(cpp_audit(snap))                                        │
│          want = canon(expected_patches(snap))                                │
│          if got != want:                                                     │
│              failures.append(f"case {i}: got {got}, want {want}")            │
│      assert not failures, "\n".join(failures[:5])                            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: When the randomized test fails, including the first few mismatched
cases in the assertion message would help debugging without exposing the full
reference implementation to the agent (since tests aren't visible at runtime).

[Container] 2026/06/27 03:27:16.119913 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found'
Our lab access gateway runs a C++ setup auditor in `/app/cpp-auditor`. It takes a
snapshot of a host's configuration files and proposes the patches needed to bring
the host into policy. It exposes `POST /v1/setup/audit` with a request body
`{"files": {...}}`, where `files` carries `passwd`, `shadow`, `group`, and
`sudoers` as raw file-content strings, `authorized_keys` as an object mapping
each username to that user's key-file text, and `sshd_config.d` as an object
mapping each drop-in filename to its text. It replies with `{"patches": [...]}`,
where every patch is an object keyed by `action`: either
`{"action": "sudoers.require_password", "target": "<user>"}`,
`{"action": "ssh.revoke_keys", "target": "<user>"}`, or
`{"action": "systemd.set_dropin", "unit": "sshd", "key": "<keyword>", "value": "<value>"}`.
Keep this request and patch shape exactly; don't rename the keys.

The authoritative policy is the **Lab Access Hardening Standard** in
`/app/docs/standard.md`. The auditor has fallen behind the Standard: it predates
several changes and also has a few outright parsing defects, so it now disagrees
with policy on many hosts. The Standard is a body of numbered controls plus an
authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G); where the body and an amendment
conflict, the amendment governs (section 1.4), so several controls don't mean
what their body text alone says. The exact token sets, accepted values, ceilings,
overrides, runas scoping, and ledger schema are defined there as amended.

The auditor's logic lives mainly in `/app/cpp-auditor/src/parse.cpp` (turns the
raw files into a normalized inventory), `/app/cpp-auditor/src/audit.cpp` (turns
that inventory into patches), and `/app/cpp-auditor/src/main.cpp` (the service
and its audit ledger). Make it conform to the Standard so that, for any host
snapshot, `POST /v1/setup/audit` returns exactly the patch set the Standard
implies; a host that is already compliant must produce an empty patch set.
Rebuild with the provided CMake setup (the existing `build/` target) after
editing.

The auditor must also be **stateless**: the patch set returned for a request must
be a pure function of the snapshot in that request, unaffected by any snapshot
audited earlier in the same run. Verification audits many different hosts against
a single long-lived server, so anything that lets one host's data carry over to
another will show up as incorrect patches.

The auditor also keeps a persistent **audit ledger** at
`/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json` and must append every patch it emits, with
earlier records preserved. Operators have noticed the ledger is no longer being
updated even though audits keep returning results. The ledger on disk is in the
older `{"schema": 1, "audits": [...]}` layout; the Standard's audit control now
requires `{"schema": 2, "entries": [...]}`. Reconcile it to that current schema,
carrying every existing record forward into `entries` (appended patches use the
same patch objects described above), so durable logging is restored.

The behaviors the auditor must get right, all governed by the Standard, are:

- **disabled-account detection** — which `shadow` password tokens, login shells,
  and account-expiry values mark an account disabled;
- **effective group membership** — including members who belong via their
  *primary* group id, not just the textual member list;
- **passwordless-sudo determination** — which `sudoers` rules actually grant it
  (the command scope `NOPASSWD` applies to, the runas target, alias/`%group`/
  negation resolution, last-match ordering, `includedir` splicing, and
  per-user/group `Defaults` overrides);
- **service-account exemptions** — the exempt roster and the user-id ceiling;
- **effective sshd drop-in settings** — the fragment precedence rules and which
  keywords and values are accepted (including the keyboard-interactive keyword
  and its deprecated alias); evaluate `Match` blocks for the fixed audit context
  of connecting user `root` from source address `198.51.100.10` on host
  `gw-lab-01`, which is also the host the `sudoers` host scoping resolves against.

The toolchain (cmake, g++) is already installed and everything runs offline.

Strengths:  The randomized property test exercises every amended clause of the

================================================================================

The test suite verifies the C++ auditor against the full resolved Lab Access
Hardening Standard using a dual strategy: 14 fixture-based regression tests
that target individual policy behaviors (disabled accounts, primary GID
membership, last-match-wins, sshd Match context, host scoping, includedir,
runas filtering, account expiry, Defaults overrides, kbd-interactive aliasing)
and a randomized property test that generates 60 distinct host snapshots at a
fixed seed and compares the auditor's output against a hidden reference
implementation. The ledger migration and statefulness requirements are also
tested. The reference implementation correctly encodes every constant,
threshold, and algorithm specified by the Standard and its 18 amendments. No
shortcut solution exists: the agent cannot access the reference, the random
test covers exponentially many code paths, and the fixtures exercise precise
expected patch sets that require correct parsing and audit logic.

[Container] 2026/06/27 03:27:14.910247 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found; task may use steps/*/instruction.md'
Our lab access gateway runs a C++ setup auditor in `/app/cpp-auditor`. It takes a
snapshot of a host's configuration files and proposes the patches needed to bring
the host into policy. It exposes `POST /v1/setup/audit` with a request body
`{"files": {...}}`, where `files` carries `passwd`, `shadow`, `group`, and
`sudoers` as raw file-content strings, `authorized_keys` as an object mapping
each username to that user's key-file text, and `sshd_config.d` as an object
mapping each drop-in filename to its text. It replies with `{"patches": [...]}`,
where every patch is an object keyed by `action`: either
`{"action": "sudoers.require_password", "target": "<user>"}`,
`{"action": "ssh.revoke_keys", "target": "<user>"}`, or
`{"action": "systemd.set_dropin", "unit": "sshd", "key": "<keyword>", "value": "<value>"}`.
Keep this request and patch shape exactly; don't rename the keys.

The authoritative policy is the **Lab Access Hardening Standard** in
`/app/docs/standard.md`. The auditor has fallen behind the Standard: it predates
several changes and also has a few outright parsing defects, so it now disagrees
with policy on many hosts. The Standard is a body of numbered controls plus an
authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G); where the body and an amendment
conflict, the amendment governs (section 1.4), so several controls don't mean
what their body text alone says. The exact token sets, accepted values, ceilings,
overrides, runas scoping, and ledger schema are defined there as amended.

The auditor's logic lives mainly in `/app/cpp-auditor/src/parse.cpp` (turns the
raw files into a normalized inventory), `/app/cpp-auditor/src/audit.cpp` (turns
that inventory into patches), and `/app/cpp-auditor/src/main.cpp` (the service
and its audit ledger). Make it conform to the Standard so that, for any host
snapshot, `POST /v1/setup/audit` returns exactly the patch set the Standard
implies; a host that is already compliant must produce an empty patch set.
Rebuild with the provided CMake setup (the existing `build/` target) after
editing.

The auditor must also be **stateless**: the patch set returned for a request must
be a pure function of the snapshot in that request, unaffected by any snapshot
audited earlier in the same run. Verification audits many different hosts against
a single long-lived server, so anything that lets one host's data carry over to
another will show up as incorrect patches.

The auditor also keeps a persistent **audit ledger** at
`/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json` and must append every patch it emits, with
earlier records preserved. Operators have noticed the ledger is no longer being
updated even though audits keep returning results. The ledger on disk is in the
older `{"schema": 1, "audits": [...]}` layout; the Standard's audit control now
requires `{"schema": 2, "entries": [...]}`. Reconcile it to that current schema,
carrying every existing record forward into `entries` (appended patches use the
same patch objects described above), so durable logging is restored.

The behaviors the auditor must get right, all governed by the Standard, are:

- **disabled-account detection** — which `shadow` password tokens, login shells,
  and account-expiry values mark an account disabled;
- **effective group membership** — including members who belong via their
  *primary* group id, not just the textual member list;
- **passwordless-sudo determination** — which `sudoers` rules actually grant it
  (the command scope `NOPASSWD` applies to, the runas target, alias/`%group`/
  negation resolution, last-match ordering, `includedir` splicing, and
  per-user/group `Defaults` overrides);
- **service-account exemptions** — the exempt roster and the user-id ceiling;
- **effective sshd drop-in settings** — the fragment precedence rules and which
  keywords and values are accepted (including the keyboard-interactive keyword
  and its deprecated alias); evaluate `Match` blocks for the fixed audit context
  of connecting user `root` from source address `198.51.100.10` on host
  `gw-lab-01`, which is also the host the `sudoers` host scoping resolves against.

The toolchain (cmake, g++) is already installed and everything runs offline.

# Copy results back and upload to S3
cat output/message.json
aws s3 cp output/message.json ${OUTPUT_S3_URI}

# Rubric 1
Agent reads and analyzes the Lab Access Hardening Standard (standard.md) including Appendix G amendments before making code changes, +3
Agent extends the disabled-account detection to include the *LK* locked-password token as specified by amendment G-2026-01, +2
Agent adds the extended non-interactive shell set (/usr/bin/git-shell, /bin/sync) to the nologin shells list per G-2026-01, +2
Agent implements account-expiry detection (shadow field 8 below reference day 20620) per G-2026-16, +3
Agent fixes effective_group_members to include users whose primary GID matches the group (not just textual member list) per AC-GROUP-EFFECTIVE, +3
Agent implements NOPASSWD:ALL-only checking so only NOPASSWD applied to the entire command set (ALL) counts, not command-specific NOPASSWD per G-2026-02, +3
Agent implements last-match-wins semantics for sudoers so a later spec supersedes earlier grants for the same principal per G-2026-07, +3
Agent implements runas scoping so that a NOPASSWD:ALL grant only counts when the rule permits running as root per G-2026-15, +2
Agent implements host scoping so sudoers specs apply only when their host field resolves to include the audit host (gw-lab-01) per G-2026-18, +2
Agent implements #includedir or @includedir splicing of sudoers.d files (sorted by name) at the directive position per G-2026-09, +2
Agent implements Defaults:<binder> !authenticate as granting passwordless sudo to the bound user/group per G-2026-05, +2
Agent implements sshd Match block evaluation against the fixed audit context (user root, address 198.51.100.10, host gw-lab-01) per G-2026-17, +3
Agent folds ChallengeResponseAuthentication as a deprecated alias of KbdInteractiveAuthentication per G-2026-06, +2
Agent adds KbdInteractiveAuthentication to the list of checked sshd keywords in audit.cpp, +2
Agent lowers the service-account UID ceiling from 999 to 499 and adds svc_backup to the exempt roster per G-2026-03, +2
Agent implements acceptance of prohibit-password and without-password values for PermitRootLogin per G-2026-04 and G-2026-10, +2
Agent removes the global account registry (g_registry) in main.cpp to restore per-request statefulness isolation, +5
Agent fixes the audit ledger to migrate from schema 1 (audits key) to schema 2 (entries key) while preserving existing records and appending new patches, +3
Agent successfully rebuilds the project with CMake after making changes, +3
Agent introduces compilation errors that prevent the project from building, -5
Agent modifies the API contract (renames keys, changes endpoint path, or alters the patch/request JSON shape), -5
Agent does not read the Appendix G amendments and implements only the body text of controls, -3

                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires the agent to fix a C++ security auditor that parses host
configuration snapshots (passwd, shadow, sudoers, sshd drop-ins) and proposes
remediation patches per a detailed Lab Access Hardening Standard. The agent
must debug parsing defects, implement missing amendment clauses (extended
shells, primary-group membership, sudoers last-match, sshd Match blocks,
audit ledger migration), and fix a statefulness bug in the HTTP server. The
test suite uses 16 deterministic fixture tests plus 60 randomized host
snapshots generated from a hidden reference implementation in helpers.py.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Unpinned pytest Version in requirements.txt
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/requirements.txt (line 14)
Problem: pytest is pinned to 8.2.2, but the task.toml and Dockerfile pattern
         suggest using a more recent version (8.4.1 is the current standard
         in the skill reference examples). This is not a critical issue since
         the version IS pinned with hashes, but it diverges from the latest
         recommended version.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  pytest==8.2.2 \                                                            │
│      --hash=sha256:c434598117762e2bd304e526244f67bf66bbd7b5d6cf22138...      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix: Consider updating to pytest==8.4.1 with fresh hashes, though
the current pinned version is functionally valid and will not cause failures.

Explanation: The version is properly pinned and hash-locked so this is a minor
freshness concern, not a correctness issue.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Random Test Determinism Relies on Seed Stability
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test_outputs.py (line 29)
Problem: The randomized test uses `random.Random(20260625)` which is
         deterministic for a given Python version but could theoretically
         produce different sequences across major Python version changes.
         The Dockerfile pins Python 3.13, so this is safe today.

Current approach: A seeded RNG generates 60 random host snapshots per run.

Suggested fix: No immediate action required — the digest-pinned base image
ensures Python 3.13 is used consistently. Document the seed dependency if
the base image is ever upgraded.

Explanation: The risk is low because the Dockerfile is digest-pinned to a
specific Python 3.13 image, but awareness helps future maintainers.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Consider Adding a Build Failure Diagnostic Test
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test_outputs.py

Current approach: test.sh handles build failures by writing reward 0 and
exiting, but there is no explicit pytest test that verifies the binary was
built successfully before hitting the HTTP endpoint.

Suggested improvement:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def test_auditor_binary_exists():                                          │
│      """The auditor binary was compiled successfully."""                     │
│      import os                                                              │
│      assert os.path.isfile("/app/cpp-auditor/build/setup_auditor")          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: This would give clearer diagnostics when the agent's code changes
introduce compile errors — currently a build failure results in reward 0 with
no pytest output, which is handled by test.sh but less informative.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is an excellent, well-crafted Terminal-Bench task that tests deep
systems-programming and policy-interpretation skills. The deliberately broken
C++ auditor with multiple interacting defects, a detailed policy document
with amendments, and a comprehensive hidden reference implementation make
this a challenging and fair evaluation of real-world debugging ability.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Comprehensive test suite with 16 fixture tests and 60 randomized hosts
    exercising every amended policy clause
  ✓ Well-designed task: broken code has realistic bugs (statefulness, missing
    features, outdated parsing) that require reading and interpreting a long
    policy document
  ✓ Proper offline setup: all deps (cmake, g++, pytest, nlohmann-json) are
    installed at build time; test.sh performs no network access
  ✓ Hidden reference implementation in helpers.py prevents agent cheating
    while ensuring correctness

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Minor: pytest version (8.2.2) slightly behind current recommended (8.4.1)

Evaluates: C++ debugging, policy/specification interpretation, systems
           security configuration, stateful service architecture

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ✅ READY TO USE

  This task is well-structured, properly configured, and exercises meaningful
  agent capabilities. The minor pytest version concern does not affect
  functionality.

│                              │                │ intentionally verbose with   │
│                              │                │ filler to make policy        │
│                              │                │ extraction require careful   │
│                              │                │ reading.                     │
├──────────────────────────────┼────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┤
│ Structured Data Schema       │ pass           │ instruction.md explicitly    │
│                              │                │ documents the exact schema:  │
│                              │                │ the request body is          │
│                              │                │ {"files": {"passwd": "...",  │
│                              │                │ "shadow": "...", "group":    │
│                              │                │ "...", "sudoers": "...",     │
│                              │                │ "authorized_keys":           │
│                              │                │ {username: text},            │
│                              │                │ "sshd_config.d": {filename:  │
│                              │                │ text}}} and the response is  │
│                              │                │ {"patches": [...]} where     │
│                              │                │ each patch is one of three   │
│                              │                │ precisely defined shapes     │
│                              │                │ with exact key names         │
│                              │                │ (action, target;             │
│                              │                │ action/unit/key/value). The  │
│                              │                │ instruction says 'Keep this  │
│                              │                │ request and patch shape      │

✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solution/solve.sh copies corrected source files (parse.cpp, audit.cpp, main.cpp) into /app/cpp-auditor/src/ and rebuilds the binary with cmake. The solution files contain genuine C++ logic implementing all the policy rules—they parse passwd/shadow/group/sudoers/sshd files, apply last-match semantics, resolve aliases, evaluate Match blocks, handle #includedir, perform schema migration, etc. This is a complete algorithmic implementation, not a hardcoded output echo.
➖ not_applicable - file_reference_mentioned: This task does not require the agent to produce output files checked by tests. The output is an HTTP response body (JSON patches). The test directly calls the HTTP endpoint and checks the returned JSON. No output files are written by the agent that tests then read. The only file the tests read is the ledger (/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json), and this path is documented in instruction.md as '/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json'.

[Container] 2026/06/27 03:27:16.119913 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found'
Our lab access gateway runs a C++ setup auditor in `/app/cpp-auditor`. It takes a
snapshot of a host's configuration files and proposes the patches needed to bring
the host into policy. It exposes `POST /v1/setup/audit` with a request body
`{"files": {...}}`, where `files` carries `passwd`, `shadow`, `group`, and
`sudoers` as raw file-content strings, `authorized_keys` as an object mapping
each username to that user's key-file text, and `sshd_config.d` as an object
mapping each drop-in filename to its text. It replies with `{"patches": [...]}`,
where every patch is an object keyed by `action`: either
`{"action": "sudoers.require_password", "target": "<user>"}`,
`{"action": "ssh.revoke_keys", "target": "<user>"}`, or
`{"action": "systemd.set_dropin", "unit": "sshd", "key": "<keyword>", "value": "<value>"}`.
Keep this request and patch shape exactly; don't rename the keys.

The authoritative policy is the **Lab Access Hardening Standard** in
`/app/docs/standard.md`. The auditor has fallen behind the Standard: it predates
several changes and also has a few outright parsing defects, so it now disagrees
with policy on many hosts. The Standard is a body of numbered controls plus an
authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G); where the body and an amendment
conflict, the amendment governs (section 1.4), so several controls don't mean
what their body text alone says. The exact token sets, accepted values, ceilings,
overrides, runas scoping, and ledger schema are defined there as amended.

The auditor's logic lives mainly in `/app/cpp-auditor/src/parse.cpp` (turns the
raw files into a normalized inventory), `/app/cpp-auditor/src/audit.cpp` (turns
that inventory into patches), and `/app/cpp-auditor/src/main.cpp` (the service
and its audit ledger). Make it conform to the Standard so that, for any host
snapshot, `POST /v1/setup/audit` returns exactly the patch set the Standard
implies; a host that is already compliant must produce an empty patch set.
Rebuild with the provided CMake setup (the existing `build/` target) after
editing.

The auditor must also be **stateless**: the patch set returned for a request must
be a pure function of the snapshot in that request, unaffected by any snapshot
audited earlier in the same run. Verification audits many different hosts against
a single long-lived server, so anything that lets one host's data carry over to
another will show up as incorrect patches.

The auditor also keeps a persistent **audit ledger** at
`/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json` and must append every patch it emits, with
earlier records preserved. Operators have noticed the ledger is no longer being
updated even though audits keep returning results. The ledger on disk is in the
older `{"schema": 1, "audits": [...]}` layout; the Standard's audit control now
requires `{"schema": 2, "entries": [...]}`. Reconcile it to that current schema,
carrying every existing record forward into `entries` (appended patches use the
same patch objects described above), so durable logging is restored.

The behaviors the auditor must get right, all governed by the Standard, are:

- **disabled-account detection** — which `shadow` password tokens, login shells,
  and account-expiry values mark an account disabled;
- **effective group membership** — including members who belong via their
  *primary* group id, not just the textual member list;
- **passwordless-sudo determination** — which `sudoers` rules actually grant it
  (the command scope `NOPASSWD` applies to, the runas target, alias/`%group`/
  negation resolution, last-match ordering, `includedir` splicing, and
  per-user/group `Defaults` overrides);
- **service-account exemptions** — the exempt roster and the user-id ceiling;
- **effective sshd drop-in settings** — the fragment precedence rules and which
  keywords and values are accepted (including the keyboard-interactive keyword
  and its deprecated alias); evaluate `Match` blocks for the fixed audit context
  of connecting user `root` from source address `198.51.100.10` on host
  `gw-lab-01`, which is also the host the `sudoers` host scoping resolves against.

The toolchain (cmake, g++) is already installed and everything runs offline.

2026-06-27 03:28:19,815 INFO scripts.test_quality_judge.reviewer: [tbench-task] agent finished (13 turns)
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite combines 14 targeted fixture tests with 60 randomized-snapshot
tests driven by a comprehensive hidden reference implementation, making it
infeasible to pass without genuinely implementing the resolved policy.

Strengths:  The randomized property test exercises every amended clause of the
Standard across diverse combinations, while dedicated fixtures isolate specific
corner cases (host scoping, runas filtering, includedir splicing, Match block
evaluation). The reference implementation in helpers.py faithfully encodes every
amendment in Appendix G, and the agent cannot access it.

Weaknesses: None that would enable a shortcut solution.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite verifies the C++ auditor against the full resolved Lab Access
Hardening Standard using a dual strategy: 14 fixture-based regression tests
that target individual policy behaviors (disabled accounts, primary GID
membership, last-match-wins, sshd Match context, host scoping, includedir,
runas filtering, account expiry, Defaults overrides, kbd-interactive aliasing)
and a randomized property test that generates 60 distinct host snapshots at a
fixed seed and compares the auditor's output against a hidden reference
implementation. The ledger migration and statefulness requirements are also
tested. The reference implementation correctly encodes every constant,
threshold, and algorithm specified by the Standard and its 18 amendments. No
shortcut solution exists: the agent cannot access the reference, the random
test covers exponentially many code paths, and the fixtures exercise precise
expected patch sets that require correct parsing and audit logic.

================================================================================

[Container] 2026/06/27 03:27:16.119913 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found'
Our lab access gateway runs a C++ setup auditor in `/app/cpp-auditor`. It takes a
snapshot of a host's configuration files and proposes the patches needed to bring
the host into policy. It exposes `POST /v1/setup/audit` with a request body
`{"files": {...}}`, where `files` carries `passwd`, `shadow`, `group`, and
`sudoers` as raw file-content strings, `authorized_keys` as an object mapping
each username to that user's key-file text, and `sshd_config.d` as an object
mapping each drop-in filename to its text. It replies with `{"patches": [...]}`,
where every patch is an object keyed by `action`: either
`{"action": "sudoers.require_password", "target": "<user>"}`,
`{"action": "ssh.revoke_keys", "target": "<user>"}`, or
`{"action": "systemd.set_dropin", "unit": "sshd", "key": "<keyword>", "value": "<value>"}`.
Keep this request and patch shape exactly; don't rename the keys.

The authoritative policy is the **Lab Access Hardening Standard** in
`/app/docs/standard.md`. The auditor has fallen behind the Standard: it predates
several changes and also has a few outright parsing defects, so it now disagrees
with policy on many hosts. The Standard is a body of numbered controls plus an
authoritative amendments appendix (Appendix G); where the body and an amendment
conflict, the amendment governs (section 1.4), so several controls don't mean
what their body text alone says. The exact token sets, accepted values, ceilings,
overrides, runas scoping, and ledger schema are defined there as amended.

The auditor's logic lives mainly in `/app/cpp-auditor/src/parse.cpp` (turns the
raw files into a normalized inventory), `/app/cpp-auditor/src/audit.cpp` (turns
that inventory into patches), and `/app/cpp-auditor/src/main.cpp` (the service
and its audit ledger). Make it conform to the Standard so that, for any host
snapshot, `POST /v1/setup/audit` returns exactly the patch set the Standard
implies; a host that is already compliant must produce an empty patch set.
Rebuild with the provided CMake setup (the existing `build/` target) after
editing.

The auditor must also be **stateless**: the patch set returned for a request must
be a pure function of the snapshot in that request, unaffected by any snapshot
audited earlier in the same run. Verification audits many different hosts against
a single long-lived server, so anything that lets one host's data carry over to
another will show up as incorrect patches.

The auditor also keeps a persistent **audit ledger** at
`/app/cpp-auditor/state/ledger.json` and must append every patch it emits, with
earlier records preserved. Operators have noticed the ledger is no longer being
updated even though audits keep returning results. The ledger on disk is in the
older `{"schema": 1, "audits": [...]}` layout; the Standard's audit control now
requires `{"schema": 2, "entries": [...]}`. Reconcile it to that current schema,
carrying every existing record forward into `entries` (appended patches use the
same patch objects described above), so durable logging is restored.

The behaviors the auditor must get right, all governed by the Standard, are:

- **disabled-account detection** — which `shadow` password tokens, login shells,
  and account-expiry values mark an account disabled;
- **effective group membership** — including members who belong via their
  *primary* group id, not just the textual member list;
- **passwordless-sudo determination** — which `sudoers` rules actually grant it
  (the command scope `NOPASSWD` applies to, the runas target, alias/`%group`/
  negation resolution, last-match ordering, `includedir` splicing, and
  per-user/group `Defaults` overrides);
- **service-account exemptions** — the exempt roster and the user-id ceiling;
- **effective sshd drop-in settings** — the fragment precedence rules and which
  keywords and values are accepted (including the keyboard-interactive keyword
  and its deprecated alias); evaluate `Match` blocks for the fixed audit context
  of connecting user `root` from source address `198.51.100.10` on host
  `gw-lab-01`, which is also the host the `sudoers` host scoping resolves against.

The toolchain (cmake, g++) is already installed and everything runs offline.

2026-06-27 03:28:19,815 INFO scripts.test_quality_judge.reviewer: [tbench-task] agent finished (13 turns)
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite combines 14 targeted fixture tests with 60 randomized-snapshot
tests driven by a comprehensive hidden reference implementation, making it
infeasible to pass without genuinely implementing the resolved policy.

Strengths:  The randomized property test exercises every amended clause of the
Standard across diverse combinations, while dedicated fixtures isolate specific
corner cases (host scoping, runas filtering, includedir splicing, Match block
evaluation). The reference implementation in helpers.py faithfully encodes every
amendment in Appendix G, and the agent cannot access it.

Weaknesses: None that would enable a shortcut solution.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite verifies the C++ auditor against the full resolved Lab Access
Hardening Standard using a dual strategy: 14 fixture-based regression tests
that target individual policy behaviors (disabled accounts, primary GID
membership, last-match-wins, sshd Match context, host scoping, includedir,
runas filtering, account expiry, Defaults overrides, kbd-interactive aliasing)
and a randomized property test that generates 60 distinct host snapshots at a
fixed seed and compares the auditor's output against a hidden reference
implementation. The ledger migration and statefulness requirements are also
tested. The reference implementation correctly encodes every constant,
threshold, and algorithm specified by the Standard and its 18 amendments. No
shortcut solution exists: the agent cannot access the reference, the random
test covers exponentially many code paths, and the fixtures exercise precise
expected patch sets that require correct parsing and audit logic.

================================================================================

# Rubric 1
Agent reads and analyzes the Lab Access Hardening Standard (standard.md) including Appendix G amendments before making code changes, +3
Agent extends the disabled-account detection to include the *LK* locked-password token as specified by amendment G-2026-01, +2
Agent adds the extended non-interactive shell set (/usr/bin/git-shell, /bin/sync) to the nologin shells list per G-2026-01, +2
Agent implements account-expiry detection (shadow field 8 below reference day 20620) per G-2026-16, +3
Agent fixes effective_group_members to include users whose primary GID matches the group (not just textual member list) per AC-GROUP-EFFECTIVE, +3
Agent implements NOPASSWD:ALL-only checking so only NOPASSWD applied to the entire command set (ALL) counts, not command-specific NOPASSWD per G-2026-02, +3
Agent implements last-match-wins semantics for sudoers so a later spec supersedes earlier grants for the same principal per G-2026-07, +3
Agent implements runas scoping so that a NOPASSWD:ALL grant only counts when the rule permits running as root per G-2026-15, +2
Agent implements host scoping so sudoers specs apply only when their host field resolves to include the audit host (gw-lab-01) per G-2026-18, +2
Agent implements #includedir or @includedir splicing of sudoers.d files (sorted by name) at the directive position per G-2026-09, +2
Agent implements Defaults:<binder> !authenticate as granting passwordless sudo to the bound user/group per G-2026-05, +2
Agent implements sshd Match block evaluation against the fixed audit context (user root, address 198.51.100.10, host gw-lab-01) per G-2026-17, +3
Agent folds ChallengeResponseAuthentication as a deprecated alias of KbdInteractiveAuthentication per G-2026-06, +2
Agent adds KbdInteractiveAuthentication to the list of checked sshd keywords in audit.cpp, +2
Agent lowers the service-account UID ceiling from 999 to 499 and adds svc_backup to the exempt roster per G-2026-03, +2
Agent implements acceptance of prohibit-password and without-password values for PermitRootLogin per G-2026-04 and G-2026-10, +2
Agent removes the global account registry (g_registry) in main.cpp to restore per-request statefulness isolation, +5
Agent fixes the audit ledger to migrate from schema 1 (audits key) to schema 2 (entries key) while preserving existing records and appending new patches, +3

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires the agent to fix a C++ security auditor that parses host
configuration snapshots (passwd, shadow, sudoers, sshd drop-ins) and proposes
remediation patches per a detailed Lab Access Hardening Standard. The agent
must debug parsing defects, implement missing amendment clauses (extended
shells, primary-group membership, sudoers last-match, sshd Match blocks,
audit ledger migration), and fix a statefulness bug in the HTTP server. The
test suite uses 16 deterministic fixture tests plus 60 randomized host
snapshots generated from a hidden reference implementation in helpers.py.

1. Unpinned pytest Version in requirements.txt
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/requirements.txt (line 14)
Problem: pytest is pinned to 8.2.2, but the task.toml and Dockerfile pattern
         suggest using a more recent version (8.4.1 is the current standard
         in the skill reference examples). This is not a critical issue since
         the version IS pinned with hashes, but it diverges from the latest
         recommended version.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  pytest==8.2.2 \                                                            │
│      --hash=sha256:c434598117762e2bd304e526244f67bf66bbd7b5d6cf22138...      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix: Consider updating to pytest==8.4.1 with fresh hashes, though
the current pinned version is functionally valid and will not cause failures.

Explanation: The version is properly pinned and hash-locked so this is a minor
freshness concern, not a correctness issue.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Random Test Determinism Relies on Seed Stability
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test_outputs.py (line 29)
Problem: The randomized test uses `random.Random(20260625)` which is
         deterministic for a given Python version but could theoretically
         produce different sequences across major Python version changes.
         The Dockerfile pins Python 3.13, so this is safe today.

Current approach: A seeded RNG generates 60 random host snapshots per run.

Suggested fix: No immediate action required — the digest-pinned base image
ensures Python 3.13 is used consistently. Document the seed dependency if
the base image is ever upgraded.

Explanation: The risk is low because the Dockerfile is digest-pinned to a
specific Python 3.13 image, but awareness helps future maintainers.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Consider Adding a Build Failure Diagnostic Test
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test_outputs.py

Current approach: test.sh handles build failures by writing reward 0 and
exiting, but there is no explicit pytest test that verifies the binary was
built successfully before hitting the HTTP endpoint.

Suggested improvement:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def test_auditor_binary_exists():                                          │
│      """The auditor binary was compiled successfully."""                     │
│      import os                                                              │
│      assert os.path.isfile("/app/cpp-auditor/build/setup_auditor")          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: This would give clearer diagnostics when the agent's code changes
introduce compile errors — currently a build failure results in reward 0 with
no pytest output, which is handled by test.sh but less informative.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is an excellent, well-crafted Terminal-Bench task that tests deep
systems-programming and policy-interpretation skills. The deliberately broken
C++ auditor with multiple interacting defects, a detailed policy document
with amendments, and a comprehensive hidden reference implementation make
this a challenging and fair evaluation of real-world debugging ability.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Comprehensive test suite with 16 fixture tests and 60 randomized hosts
    exercising every amended policy clause
  ✓ Well-designed task: broken code has realistic bugs (statefulness, missing
    features, outdated parsing) that require reading and interpreting a long
    policy document
  ✓ Proper offline setup: all deps (cmake, g++, pytest, nlohmann-json) are
    installed at build time; test.sh performs no network access
  ✓ Hidden reference implementation in helpers.py prevents agent cheating
    while ensuring correctness

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Minor: pytest version (8.2.2) slightly behind current recommended (8.4.1)

Evaluates: C++ debugging, policy/specification interpretation, systems
           security configuration, stateful service architecture

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ✅ READY TO USE

  This task is well-structured, properly configured, and exercises meaningful
  agent capabilities. The minor pytest version concern does not affect
  functionality.
================================================================================
